item.title: The Truth Behind That Tucker Carlson Apology Video
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex. In tonight’s edition, we take a deep dive into Tucker Carlson’s mea culpa for “misleading people” into voting for Trump – and why…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex. In tonight’s edition, we take a deep dive into Tucker Carlson’s mea culpa for “misleading people” into voting for Trump – and why progressives shouldn’t just take it at face value. Plus, we ask: with Trump’s approval numbers sinking to new lows, is it just a matter of time before Fox abandons him?

You do not, under any circumstances, got to hand it to Tucker Carlson.

The former Fox star has increasingly drawn plaudits from progressives for his “credible anti-war voice” and outspoken criticism of Israel’s genocide in Gaza, resulting in Donald Trump unceremoniously excommunicating his one-time close ally and adviser from MAGA.

With Democratic lawmakers talking up a potential bipartisan alliance that would feature Carlson, the far-right pundit sparked even more applause from the left when he issued an apology for “misleading people” with his past advocacy for Trump.

“You know, we’ll be tormented by it for a long time – I will be… It was not intentional,” Carlson declared at the tail-end of a two-plus-hour podcast conversation with his brother, former Trump speechwriter Buckley Carlson, whom he colloquially referred to as “Uncle Buck.”

“You and I and everyone else who supported him – you wrote speeches for him, I campaigned for him – I mean, we’re implicated in this for sure,” Tucker Carlson said. “It’s not enough to say, ‘Well, I changed my mind’ – or like, ‘Oh, this is bad – I’m out.’”

To be certain, Tucker’s explicit condemnation of Trump’s illegal war against Iran and Israel’s genocidal violence against Palestinians should be commended. It is clearly a good thing, and we can only hope that more on the right follow his lead on these particular issues.

However, I watched the entire two-plus-hour conversation, and it was readily apparent that the two had become disenchanted with Trump precisely because they believed he wasn’t extreme and fascistic enough, and they felt the president didn’t make good on his vows of retribution against liberals and Democrats.

Besides drawing copious headlines that hyped his mea culpa for helping Trump get elected, prominent left-wing personalities praised Carlson. Even Mehdi – who also complimented Carlson recently for challenging an interviewer to define the phrase “Israel’s right to exist” – briefly gave Carlson some kudos. That is, until he listened to the rest of the conversation that took place between the two ultra-conservative brothers.

“Sorry, I screwed up late last night by posting a clip in which Tucker Carlson finally apologized for backing Trump, but I didn’t hear the disgusting language used by the other speaker,” Mehdi tweeted on Tuesday.

Indeed, shortly after Carlson expressed how torn up he was for supporting Trump, “Uncle Buck” chimed in with some conspiracy mongering about the Trump assassination attempts before referring to former Democratic presidential nominee Kamala Harris as, and I am not making this up, “Cackling Cameltoe.”

The thing is, that wasn’t the first time Buckley had used that grossly misogynistic slur when talking about the ex-veep during the chat with his brother.

It was also just the tip of the iceberg when it came to the rank homophobia, racism, xenophobia, anti-vax sentiment, conspiracy theories, and just outright repulsive behavior on display in the podcast.

Asked what his breaking point with Trump was, Buckley said it wasn’t just the Iran war, but also the failure of Trump to properly “hold to account” all of the right-wing’s boogeymen from the past decade of ragebait, which included: COVID vaccines, January 6, the George Floyd protests, Joe Biden’s preemptive pardons, Biden family corruption, the Russia probe, the autopen “scandal,” and Barack Obama being “guilty of treason,” among others.

“It is an offense against you and me and everyone we know,” Buckley fumed.

The two siblings also seemed obsessed with how “super gay” Washington is, all while tossing out wild accusations about the sexual orientations of Hillary Clinton, Barack Obama, Lindsey Graham, and Harris’s husband Doug Emhoff. According to the pair, this was all proof of the “demonic influence” that inflicts powerful politicians, which they linked to homosexuality and secret “sodomites” in DC.

And there was more. So much more. Here is just a small, non-comprehensive sampling of what else was said:

“It was not his race to lose, it was ours – and he did it on our behalf,” Tucker on John McCain.

“He hates whites,” Tucker on Obama.

“You didn’t take the vax. Thank god!” Tucker to Buckley.

“No wonder doctors are the worst people in America,” Tucker said, talking about doctors taking mRNA vaccines. He also called COVID vaccines “poison” and a “bioweapon” that “killed hundreds of thousands of people” around the world, and how Trump took the vaccine and still hasn’t apologized for doing that.

“They’re representative of America and Americans. The best kind of people,” Buckley on the January 6 insurrectionists.

“It was an operation to get rid of white cops,” Tucker said on the George Floyd protests. The pair also bemoaned Trump for failing to “exercise his power” to quell the riots and free the “wrongfully imprisoned” Derek Chauvin, the former police officer convicted of murdering Floyd.

Perhaps with his reprehensible views – which are the same as they ever were – laid out like this, progressives will think twice before looking to join forces with Tucker.

As Nicole Hemmer wrote for MS NOW earlier this month, “shining a laudatory spotlight on Carlson” and other MAGA conspiracists who have split with Trump “does their work for them, lending their reputations new legitimacy the next time they attack immigrants or promote authoritarians.”

But Cenk Uygur, the founder of The Young Turks, offered a counterargument this week amid rising backlash from some on the left over making common cause with Tucker.

“Everyone rejecting @TuckerCarlson’s apology for backing Trump - what the fuck else do you want? If you don’t think it’s good enough that he rejected Trump and joined the anti-war, anti-genocide side, you’re a bit of an asshole,” he tweeted on Tuesday.

And after Mehdi brought up the “horrible” positions Tucker endorsed throughout the latest podcast, noting “some of us can’t get behind” that, Uygur pushed back: “Did I miss the requirement to agree with all of his positions in order to accept his help in combating war and genocide?”

The truth is that when it comes to Tucker Carlson, we’ve all “seen this movie before,” as CNN’s Andrew Kirell noted on Tuesday, pointing out that the Dominion case revealed Carlson privately said in 2021 that he hated Trump “passionately.” However, by 2024, not only was Tucker fully back in Trump’s inner circle, but he was also one of the driving forces behind JD Vance’s selection as running mate.

In the end, this is all just a calculated approach by Carlson to not only get ahead of the rest of the right-wing media ecosystem in distancing themselves from an increasingly unpopular president, but also to elevate and rebrand himself as the leader of a new post-Trump conservative movement.

On the other end of the MAGA media spectrum from Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly, Alex Jones, and the rest of the new anti-Trump right sits Fox.

The conservative cable giant has steadfastly remained in the president’s corner even as Trump’s approval ratings are in freefall amid economic concerns and his illegal war of choice in Iran. Of course, it was Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ who helped push the commander-in-chief to attack Iran in the first place.

Still, with just 32% of Americans approving of Trump’s job performance – which is approaching late-stage George W. Bush lows – I wondered if we were getting close to the breaking point for Fox, especially if the midterm elections are a complete wipeout for the GOP.

Also, it isn’t as if we haven’t seen the Rupert Murdoch-owned network – which has long served as the de facto communications arm of the Republican Party – try to dump Trump before. Following his 2020 election loss, Fox repeatedly tried to move on from Trump, even enacting a “soft ban” amid the Dominion lawsuit over the election denialism they peddled on his behalf.

Additionally, it’s no secret that Murdoch has long had a love-hate relationship with the president and tried desperately to find a new GOP standardbearer ahead of the 2024 election, to no avail. (Things have grown only more strained between Murdoch and Trump following the president’s lawsuit against the Wall Street Journal.)

Anyway, to get to the bottom of this, I decided to ask one of the only people I know who has watched more Fox News than I have over the past decade – Media Matters senior fellow Matt Gertz.

“I think it would take a seismic change in the polling, the likes of which we have not seen yet, because even as he is incredibly unpopular, Trump remains above 80, sometimes 85% among Republicans,” Gertz told me. “Those are your hardcore Fox viewers… and that is the thing that keeps the network from pivoting, and that’s what we’ve seen again and again over the years.”

Gertz pointed out that when Fox initially “thought he was toast” after his 2020 election defeat and tried to move on, many of their viewers revolted and began ditching the network for more Trump-friendly options like Newsmax, forcing Fox to “pivot really hard into election-denial stuff” to win them back.

“And you know, that ended up costing them $787 million from the Dominion settlement. But I think my read is that they viewed it as cheap at that price because they get to keep the viewers coming back,” he added.

Another point Gertz raised is that over the past few years, Fox has largely rid itself of Trump-skeptical voices, so the on-air talent willing to consistently push back against the president is largely absent.

“They purged the news side, and all the Chris Wallace, Shep Smith, Neil Cavuto types have been pushed out,” Gertz said. “Steve Doocy became basically the most prominent sometimes-skeptic after that, and he’s been put out to pasture.”

In other words, the bottom line is that we likely won’t see Fox distance itself from Trump any time soon, as it is fully captured by its audience.

“They recognize that they had basically had a near-death experience after the 2020 election, and what they learned from that was, even if it requires them to tell lies that are incredibly expensive in the realm of hundreds of millions of dollars, it’s in their interest to do that,” Gertz added.

“They’re in so deep at this point,” he concluded. “They’ve defended him through multiple impeachments, an insurrection, and incredible amounts of corruption over the past year. They’re staffing the administration. Sean Hannity retains a fairly powerful post. Laura Ingraham’s in business with Donald Trump Jr. I mean, they’re just in too deep at this point.”

A Fox News representative did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: Warnings For Americans From A Historian of the Nazis
item.description: Donald Trump’s musings about dictatorship once sounded almost funny. Nobody laughs anymore. Trump has not created a dictatorship. He has, however, dragged the US further toward authoritarian rule than many of us thought possible. His corruption of the legal system, subordination of Congress,…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump’s musings about dictatorship once sounded almost funny. Nobody laughs anymore.

Trump has not created a dictatorship. He has, however, dragged the US further toward authoritarian rule than many of us thought possible. His corruption of the legal system, subordination of Congress, embrace of state violence, and hostility toward free elections led the sober-minded centrist Jonathan Rauch to write earlier this year: “Yes, it’s fascism.”

How Trump has managed to do that, contravening the values that made the US the world’s leading democracy, remains hard to fathom. But at least part of the answer lies in his use of techniques that earlier historical figures have used to amass and centralize power.

Laurence Rees, a former head of history at the BBC and best-selling author, has made studying those techniques his career’s work. His books and video documentaries explore how skillful, if repugnant, Nazi leadership drove ordinary Germans to monstrous wrongdoing during World War II. In January 2025, as Trump returned to the White House, Rees published ‘The Nazi Mind’ in an attempt to explain the inexplicable.

To state the obvious, Donald Trump is not Adolf Hitler. MAGA is not Nazism. No despot or movement in history compares to the Third Reich, and the nature and scale of evil it perpetrated.

Moreover, Trump’s handiwork appears less formidable today. Most of America recoils from his second-term grotesquerie. If permitted free and fair elections, voters will punish his Republican Party this fall – just as Hungarian voters have now punished Trump’s authoritarian role model Viktor Orbán.

But the lessons Rees identifies remain relevant to the crisis confronting 21st-century America. And that is not merely because Trump and a disturbing number of his young fans have at times expressed admiration for Nazi strength and ruthlessness.

“Everything is fragile – often a great deal more fragile than we think,” Rees writes. He lists 12 “warning signs” of how authoritarians can corrupt democracy.

All sound familiar.

Spreading conspiracy theories. Nazis persuaded rank-and-file Germans that their problems stemmed from the machinations of Jews. You can hear unmistakable echoes as Trump factotums blame immigrants for violent crime, stolen tax money, even high housing costs. “The mass looting of a civilization,” spits White House propagandist Stephen Miller.

Using “them” and “us.” Would-be dictators rouse supporters by exploiting their primal instinct to sort friends from foes, scapegoating those who seem different. Trump’s rants and social media feeds show this.

Leading as a hero. Myths of heroism helped elevate authoritarian leaders from Napoleon to Hitler to Castro. Having created an image of business success, Trump promotes fantasies about his personal qualities by invoking aspirations for the Nobel Peace Prize and Congressional Medal of Honor; he depicted himself as Jesus on Truth Social.

Deploying the photograph of Trump bloodied with a raised fist after a 2024 assassination attempt, he and his supporters have declared he was “saved by God” to save America.

Corrupting youth. Those under 25, their brains not fully matured, show particular vulnerability to the lure of extremism. The conservative writer Rod Dreher has estimated that 30% or more of Gen Z Republican operatives identify with Groypers. Trump has sprinkled such Nazi-adjacent loons through his administration.

Andrew, a 20-year-old white Christian landscaper, told the conservative City Journal last year, “Hitler was a nationalist. He was like, we have to take Germany back for Germans, and I feel like we should do that for America.”

Conniving with the elite. “Dictators frequently do not seize control on their own,” Rees says. “Instead, power is handed to them by others.” Among those empowering Trump: tech billionaires, Wall Street executives, Republican congressional leaders, the conservative-led Supreme Court.

Attacking human rights. Authoritarians make subverting the rule of law and curbing press freedom job number one. Trump and his administration have tried hard on multiple fronts, from threatening regulatory punishment of news networks to denying due process to arrested immigrants to ginning up baseless prosecutions of political adversaries.

Exploiting faith. Promises of a utopian future resembling an imaginary past represent a critical tool for rallying adherents. Thus Trump, behind a facade of religious inspiration, constantly predicts America’s return to a “golden age” that allegedly existed once upon a time.

Valuing enemies. Identification of hostile forces binds authoritarian movements together. Trump and MAGA use labels such as “domestic terrorists,” “Communists,” and “radical left, country-hating Democrats.”

Eliminating resistance. Would-be dictators often face institutional opposition from the church and the military, among others. Trump seeks to cow the Vatican, while Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth weeds out generals for insufficient loyalty. The administration applies legal pressure to Democratic lawmakers and progressive organizations that challenge him.

Escalating racism. The modern GOP assembled its national majority with white conservatives repelled by the Democrats’ embrace of civil rights. Trump and prominent followers have taken today’s racial politics to another level by asserting the inherent superiority of white societies in the style of 20th-century eugenicists – who included Hitler.

Killing at a distance. Violence up close is harder to commit than at physical remove. Trump and his aides revel in aerial strikes on boats in international waters or targets in Iran, as if playing video games.

Stoking fear. “One of the most potent political statements of all is, ‘Be frightened, they are coming for your homes and children,’” Rees concludes. Trump has sounded that theme since he rode down the golden escalator in 2015.

Trump has used that bracing playbook to damage our country and the world. But Rees concludes his book on a note of optimism, which should hearten those seeking to reclaim America’s democratic heritage.

“Adolf Hitler employed every one of the techniques above,” he writes. “But in the end – at enormous human cost – something tremendous happened. The Nazis were defeated.”

John Harwood is the former chief Washington correspondent for CNBC and White House correspondent for CNN. He has interviewed every president from George H.W. Bush to Joe Biden. Sign up for ‘The Stakes with John Harwood’ to get all of his columns in your inbox.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: Good News for Democrats – and democrats
item.description: On this day in 1954, four years into the vicious and repressive “Red Scare” in American politics, Senator Joseph McCarthy began hearings into supposed communist infiltration of the US Army. Televised, the hearings exposed the far-right Wisconsin Republican to public revulsion, ultimately causing…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1954, four years into the vicious and repressive “Red Scare” in American politics, Senator Joseph McCarthy began hearings into supposed communist infiltration of the US Army. Televised, the hearings exposed the far-right Wisconsin Republican to public revulsion, ultimately causing his downfall. How times have changed…

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Wednesday wherever you are. We have just published our long-awaited profile of Hasan Piker – one of the most prominent left-wing commentators in the US who is now becoming one of the most targeted. I traveled to Los Angeles to spend time with Piker and gain a better understanding of him, beneath all the outrage. The result was a deep, meticulous, and fair profile you will not find anywhere else. Read it here.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Virginia voters punch back against the GOP in the redistricting arms race, another Democratic candidate flip-flops on Israel’s genocide, and Republicans desperately try whipping up another faux-outrage cycle while the Trump administration fumbles Iran war negotiations… again.

On Tuesday night, Virginians voted to approve a redistricting measure that will likely help Democrats flip multiple Republican-held seats in the upcoming midterms.

The measure, which voters passed by a roughly two-point margin, will, if approved by the state legislature a second time, allow lawmakers to redraw congressional districts before the midterm elections in November. The redrawn maps may shift Virginia’s congressional delegation from 6-5 to a whopping 10-1 in favor of Democrats.

Nearly $100 million of largely dark money was spent on the election, much of it in support of the measure.

The new maps are part of an array of redistricting battles set off nationwide last year, after President Donald Trump urged Republican states to carve out more GOP-friendly districts in order to hold onto the party’s narrow House majority. Texas, Missouri, and North Carolina answered Trump’s call, while Ohio was already in the process of creating new maps that will likely favor Republicans even more.

California legislators and voters punched back, creating new maps that could at least counter new Republican seats in Texas.

Florida is up next. Governor Ron DeSantis has invoked a special legislative session this month, focused on redistricting. After Tuesday night’s result, House Democratic Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries issued a warning to DeSantis.

“This war is not over. Next week, Ron DeSantis is hauling the Florida legislature back into a special session to redraw maps because Republicans know they are on the verge of an epic defeat in November,” Jeffries said in a statement.

“If Florida Republicans proceed with this illegal scheme, they will only create more prime pick-up opportunities for Democrats, just as they did with Trump’s dummymander in Texas.”

Jeffries said his party “will aggressively target for defeat” Florida Republicans Mario Díaz-Balart, Maria Elvira Salazar, Carlos Giménez, Kat Cammack, Anna Paulina Luna, Laurel Lee, Cory Mills, and Brian Mast.

“We are prepared to take them all on, and we are prepared to win,” he concluded. “Maximum warfare, everywhere, all the time.”

As it stands, Republicans are in deep trouble. Generic ballot averages have them down by between five and six points, and there’s no sign of conditions improving for the party as the top of the ticket continues to depress enthusiasm.

Four polls released Tuesday showed Trump hitting or tying second-term lows.

The American Research Group showed Trump at a dismal 32% approval – among the lowest approval ratings a US president has ever had, and the lowest the poll has shown Trump hitting in either term.

Conditions are so bad for Republicans that the Senate is in play despite what was previously seen as an unfavorable set of elections for Democrats. Democrats are now slightly favored in North Carolina and Georgia, while Ohio, Nebraska, Iowa, and even Texas are in the conversation.

Everything the GOP appeared to think it would own upon Trump’s return – from immigration and the economy, to foreign policy and the Epstein files – has instead turned horribly toxic for the party, due to its own flailing approach.

In response, the party is not adapting at all, or trying to earn back the public’s trust. Instead, it is urgently pursuing radical voter suppression proposals like the so-called “SAVE America Act,” and flirting with sending masked ICE agents to terrorize and intimidate people at the polls.

Republicans are also frantically trying to whip up outrage cycles, focused, for example, on the Twitch streamer Hasan Piker, or by pretending that a sarcastic tweet from Senator Chris Murphy was cheerleading for Iran.

Republicans have no juice in the polls. They are flopping on every single issue (including ones like immigration, which they had assumed were among their crutch issues!). It’s no wonder that the only initiative they have left is to try stealing an election once again.

Death tolls:

Iran: An Iranian official said this week that the death toll from US-Israeli strikes is at 3,375, including 383 children. It was unclear whether the death toll also included military and security forces.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,294 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

IRGC attacks ships: Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps seized at least two ships in the Strait of Hormuz, state media reported. The IRGC said the ships “operated without authorization, repeatedly violated regulations, manipulated navigational aid systems and sought to covertly exit the Strait of Hormuz, endangering maritime security.” AP, citing semiofficial Iranian media, reported that Iran attacked a third ship later Wednesday.

Talks talk: Amid uncertainty that ultimately stopped JD Vance from traveling to Pakistan, the New York Times reported that Iranian officials simply don’t trust Donald Trump, arising from his decision to rip up the nuclear deal in 2018.

Ceasefire extended: Trump announced an extension at the request of Pakistan, until Tehran “come[s] up with a unified proposal” and negotiations are over. Trump is continuing to order a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Senate storm: The right-wing outrage machine revved up over a post in which Senator Chris Murphy wrote “awesome” over news of Iranian ships beating the blockade. “I guess I just have to be more careful about sarcasm on Twitter,” the Democrat told Fox.

Lebanon strikes: Hezbollah said it fired rockets and drones into northern Israel, citing ceasefire violations by Israeli forces, which the Lebanese group said hit civilians and destroyed homes. Israel said it hit the rocket launcher and intercepted a drone. Al Jazeera reported that Israel continued to hit residential areas in southern Lebanon today.

Statue sentences: Two Israeli soldiers were jailed for 30 days for smashing a sculpture of Jesus in a Christian village in southern Lebanon, an act that caused worldwide revulsion. Israel also replaced the statue.

Jasmeet Bains, a state lawmaker running for Congress in California, has been endorsed by Democratic Majority for Israel, a pro-Israel super PAC. Now, she’s walking back her previous comment acknowledging that Israel has committed genocide in Gaza.

On a Zoom call with Fresno County Young Democrats earlier this year, Bains was asked simply: “Is Israel committing genocide in Gaza?” She responded: “Yes.”

This week, Bains denied the genocide, with her campaign telling Politico she misunderstood the question.

“I approach the word ‘genocide’ with care, and I don’t believe it applies to Israel,” Bains said in a statement. “We need leaders in Congress who approach these issues with the seriousness and thoughtfulness they deserve – not soundbites or false choices – and that will continue to be my focus.”

Bains is running against Randy Villegas, who acknowledges Israel’s genocide in Gaza. He has been endorsed by Bernie Sanders.

Pure betrayal: Trump is considering sending more than 1,000 Afghans who worked for the US during the war in their country but have been denied resettlement to the Democratic Republic of Congo, the New York Times reported.

Boat strike: Ecuadorean fishermen described to The Guardian their horrific experience after a US drone attack, including their arrest, maltreatment, removal to El Salvador, and release without charge, amid strikes supposedly aimed at drug runners that have killed nearly 200.

Political prosecution: The Southern Poverty Law Center, which monitors far-right groups, was indicted on federal fraud charges related to payments to informants. Long a target of Republican attacks, the SPLC denies wrongdoing.

Da Do Ron, Don: Trump says Ron DeSantis is “begging” for a job, according to Axios. The Florida governor, thrashed by Trump in 2024, reportedly has his eye on attorney general, defense secretary, or even - don’t laugh -a seat on the Supreme Court.

Dem out: Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick of Florida quit the US House, heading off motions to expel over alleged misuse of federal funds, which she denies.

Pac man: AIPAC’s super PAC is spending nearly $800,000 on ads targeting Republican Thomas Massie for voting with liberal Democrats on Israel.

What, in 2023, was Florida Governor Ron DeSantis forced to say he could not recall doing on a 2019 flight from Tallahassee to Washington, DC?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel killed seven Palestinians across the Gaza Strip yesterday, including a child in Jabalia Camp. Several were injured, Palestinian media reported. Israel killed another man in Jabalia today. Meanwhile, in the occupied West Bank, Israeli forces reportedly fired on mourners attending the funeral of two Palestinians killed in yesterday’s settler attack on a Palestinian school.

🇳🇬 Coup attempt: The Nigerian government charged six people for allegedly plotting to overthrow President Bola Tinubu, only a few months after a coup was said to have been thwarted. A retired general and a police inspector are among the suspects.

🇪🇺🇭🇺 Legal victory: The European Court of Justice struck down a Hungarian law banning “promotion of homosexuality” to minors.

🇯🇵 Arms sales: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said Japan will lift a decades-long ban on exporting lethal weapons, as it moves away from pacifist policies. (Nothing at all to be afraid of, right?)

🇵🇪 Corvetto out: Peru election chief Piero Corvetto resigned amid delayed results from last week’s election, hoping to “generate a climate of greater confidence.”


It is truly insane that the man who wants to be, and who Trump wants to be, the next chair of the Federal Reserve cannot say who lost the 2020 election. IT IS A CULT.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: A Palestinian School Day Ended With Israeli Settlers’ Gunfire. A Boy Was Killed, Just as His Father Was 7 Years Ago
item.description: This article was published in collaboration with Egab. AL MUGHAYYIR, the occupied West Bank – The morning at Al-Mughayyir Boys’ Secondary School had begun the way school mornings do. Two hundred students between the ages of 6 and 17 were in their classrooms, teachers at their boards, notebooks…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
This article was published in collaboration with Egab.

AL MUGHAYYIR, the occupied West Bank – The morning at Al-Mughayyir Boys’ Secondary School had begun the way school mornings do. Two hundred students between the ages of 6 and 17 were in their classrooms, teachers at their boards, notebooks open. At around noon, the sound that ended it was not the bell.

The first shot came without warning. Within seconds, students were throwing themselves behind desks, pressing against walls, or freezing where they stood. Teachers moved fast, locking doors, pulling children away from windows, turning classrooms into holding rooms where the only lesson was staying still and quiet.

Principal Bassam Abu Assaf had dealt with settler incursions before. Groups arriving with clubs, threats, intimidation.

“This was the first time there was direct gunfire,” he tells me. “They intended to harm the students. The fire was direct and heavy.” His first instinct was not to call for help but to get word to parents. A voice message went out through messaging apps: Come now. Take your children.

Abu Assaf says he feared calling for help first would take too long, time that could put the students in even more danger. Unfortunately, he could not know what was waiting for students outside the school when they tried to reach their parents.

The school sits on the edge of Al-Mughayyir village, east of Ramallah in the occupied West Bank, surrounded by open land, exposed on all sides. Three settlers, accompanied by three Israeli soldiers, according to witness accounts, had positioned themselves near the western wall – a two-meter (6.5-foot) concrete barrier topped by another two meters of mesh – and were firing toward the building and the courtyard. When parents began arriving, and students poured out to find them. That’s when the shooting intensified. The courtyard, meant to be the escape route, became the killing ground.

Aws Hamdi Al-Naasan was 14 years old and in ninth grade. He did not make it across the courtyard. He did not reach the gate.

His father’s uncle, Faraj Naasan, watched from the window of his house overlooking the school. He heard the shots, saw the chaos, and understood what was happening before he could fully process it. He had seen something like this before, not as a distant news event, but in his own family.

Aws’s father, Hamdi, was killed by Israeli settlers in 2019. He had gone out during a settler attack to help carry the wounded, managed to evacuate two injured people, and was shot while trying to reach a third. Now his son had been killed in the same village, in the same kind of attack, more than seven years later.

“This is a living embodiment of history repeating itself,” Faraj says.

Also killed in the school attack on Tuesday was Jihad Marzouq Abu Naiz, a 32-year-old resident of the village who had rushed to the school when he heard what was happening, trying to help get students out. He had left work the moment word spread. He did not come back.

Five students were wounded, some critically, according to the Palestinian Health Ministry. Ambulances could not get through the chaos quickly enough, and parents loaded injured children into their own cars. At the hospital, Aws was declared dead. Minutes later, so was Jihad.

Inside the school, a teacher from Al Naasan family, who asked not to give his full name, says what happened was “beyond what any school can bear.”

“The children who walked out today,” he tells me, “carried something with them that would not leave quickly. This trauma will live with them forever.”

Abu Assaf, recounting the morning, kept returning to the same word: the fire was direct. Not stray, not incidental. “They intended to harm the students,” he says again. In 22 years of teaching, this was not something he had prepared for.

Inside the school, blood stained the courtyard, notebooks were left open on desks. Chairs were still pushed back from where students had scrambled and run. But the violence in the occupied West Bank didn’t stop at the school.

Palestine’s Foreign Ministry condemned in the strongest terms, describing the assault on Al-Mughayyir school as a massacre carried out by settler gangs “in full coordination with the occupation army.”

“Israel’s continued contempt for the international consensus rejecting settler-colonialism, and the absence of any real accountability, encourages further crimes against our people and reflects the international community’s inability to impose its will in applying international law,” the ministry said.

The Israeli military claimed, without evidence, that a reservist had opened fire near the area on individuals who were throwing rocks toward the vehicle he was in, according to Israeli media. Soldiers were then deployed to the area. It added that it was investigating the incident and “its findings will be forwarded to the authorities.” As Palestinians know all too well, such investigations almost never produce any prosecutions, and settlers and Israeli soldiers act with impunity.

With the deaths of Al-Naasan and Abu Naim, the toll in the occupied West Bank rose to four killed by Israeli violence since morning. The Palestinian Ministry of Health also announced the death of Mohammed Majdi Al-Jaabari, 16, struck by a settler vehicle in Hebron, and Rajaa Fadl Beitawi, 49, who died from gunshot wounds sustained during an Israeli forces raid in Jenin approximately two years ago.

According to the Wall and Settlement Resistance Commission, Israeli forces and settlers carried out a combined 1,819 attacks in March alone – 1,322 by Israeli forces and 497 by settlers. The attacks were concentrated in Hebron governorate with 321 incidents, followed by Nablus with 315, Ramallah and Al-Bireh with 292, and Jerusalem with 203 – figures the commission described as “evidence of systematic, concentrated targeting across the West Bank”.

The village of Al-Mughayyir has seen repeated settler attacks in recent years. For the school, until now, the violence had stopped at the gate. This morning it did not, and a boy who had grown up without his father died the same way his father did, in the same dust, under the same sky.

Aseel Mafarjeh is a West Bank-focused journalist based in Ramallah, focusing on local stories that speak of the challenges and creativity of youth in Palestine.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: Inside the Heart and Mind of Hasan Piker
item.description: A note from our Editor-in-Chief: Prem traveled to Los Angeles to speak with and shadow Hasan Piker, one of the biggest, most controversial leftist voices in the US, who streams to hundreds of thousands of people every day on the platform Twitch. We sought to get a deeper understanding of the man…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
A note from our Editor-in-Chief:

Prem traveled to Los Angeles to speak with and shadow Hasan Piker, one of the biggest, most controversial leftist voices in the US, who streams to hundreds of thousands of people every day on the platform Twitch. We sought to get a deeper understanding of the man who has recently been thrust into the center of American politics.

This is an important and lengthy piece from Prem that we here at Zeteo believe reveals deeper insights into a progressive voice that the mainstream media, the Democratic establishment, and, of course, the Republican Party have relentlessly smeared and demonized. Please do subscribe to Zeteo for full access to this fascinating profile of Hasan Piker, and to help us do more independent journalism like this.

– Mehdi

At the turn of the century, 9-year-old Hasan Piker sat at his classroom desk in Ankara, Turkey, giving his teacher a headache. He was hyperactive, energetic, and disinterested in simply copying down what the teacher instructed him to. He liked to push back and argue. All of his teachers would have a field day with his mother, whenever they saw her.

“I was an annoying little shithead,” he tells me. It’s a moniker some of his opponents might say still applies more than a quarter century later.

He reminisced on his childhood as we sat in his LA home studio – a lair brimming with politics and gaming and culture and graphic art, all backdropping where he streams to hundreds of thousands around the world.

Back in the third grade in Turkey, Piker recalls, his provocative nature came out during a religion class. “I remember debating my teacher,” he says. “I was like, ‘Why are we only learning about Islam, if this is religion class? Why are we not learning about any other religions?’” He bristled at what felt like “indoctrination.” And so Piker’s relationship to faith began to develop into what it is today, two decades later: “non-existent,” he says, beyond identifying as culturally Muslim.

His “contract” with a higher power, if one exists at all, is to just pursue good, he says, musing that if there is an omnipotent being, they likely won’t punish someone who tried to do their best, but also consumed pork or alcohol. Not that he’s trying to do good for rewards in the afterlife, he notes.

But Piker’s childhood streak for challenging conventions persists. So much so that the entire US political establishment is now targeting him, depicting him as a radical extremist and terrorist sympathizer.

Today, Piker is one of the most prominent leftist political commentators in the US, and streams to hundreds of thousands of people every day on the platform Twitch. He has become a lightning rod – a functional Public Enemy Number One in the eyes of both major political parties.

As US-Israeli violence ravages Palestine, Lebanon, and Iran, cable networks like CNN and Fox have dedicated copious amounts of airtime to shaming this one streamer. CNN anchor Dana Bash falsely said that he excuses sexual violence. Republican politicians have followed suit. Florida Congressman Randy Fine called Piker a “jihadist.” Vice President JD Vance compared him to cannibal serial killer Jeffrey Dahmer. Florida Senator Rick Scott has said Piker “hates America” and “glorifies terrorism.” Democrats aren’t far behind. Illinois Congressman Brad Schneider called Piker an “unapologetic antisemite” while Michigan Democratic Senate candidate Mallory McMorrow compared Piker to the white supremacist, Holocaust-denying commentator Nick Fuentes.

Piker calls the comparison to Fuentes “totally indecent.” It’s among the more common smears he said he’s received: “being compared to an avowed neo-Nazi, white supremacist, whose worldview I find to be morally repugnant, whose opinions I have spent my entire life combating – opinions just like that.”

In many ways, the cancellation campaign against Piker isn’t about what he believes, what he’s said, or what he’s meant. It’s about marginalizing the left, and stymying the spread of growing anti-Israel sentiment. That doesn’t make the personal smears less painful, or the ensuing death threats less real.

None of that, though, is stopping him.

It’s Saturday, and Piker’s set for another routine day of streaming. I arrive at his home slightly before him. His assistant buzzes me in through an entry gate. Piker’s dog, Kaya, bounds out to greet me, and leads me inside his swanky home.

The first thing I see upon entering is a large office that looks like a cable news commentator’s studio meets comic book store meets Democratic Socialists of America (DSA). Political memorabilia is strewn everywhere, sewn between shelves of books and streaming awards and video games and basketballs and figurines and artistic renderings of Piker and Kaya.

Minutes later, the towering Piker walks in and greets me, briefly. He fires up his computer, opens the Twitch livestream waiting room, freshens up, and promptly returns back to his desk. “What’s up folks, I’m live and alive,” he announces to his Instagram followers, his common refrain to welcome viewers into his livestream.

As people file in by the thousands, Piker begins by sharing snippets of his personal life for a few minutes. After he hosted progressive Rep. Ilhan Omar on his stream the previous evening, he says, he went straight to help host a screening of ‘The Voice of Hind Rajab,’ a film about a child murdered in Gaza by Israeli forces. He celebrated his grandmother’s birthday that morning, in lieu of working out.

“There’s not really too much going on in my life other than those things, cause as you already know, I’m a chud-ass loser,” he quips.

With split monitors, one eye is trained onto the chat; one hand monitors the chat’s pace, highlighting comments he’ll respond to; another part of his brain toggles between serious political analysis, jokes, expletives, and historical recall.

Piker goes from discussing the Israeli government extracting sperm from deceased Israeli soldiers and civilians (a practice he said is motivated by a “eugenicist, fascist, ethno-supremacist, religious supremacist mentality”); to laughing at a clip of scholar Norman Finkelstein eviscerating Donald Trump and his administration; to reacting to Sabrina Carpenter’s confused response to a fan’s zaghrouta during her show; to joking about a fan-made TikTok about him; to analyzing US-Iran ceasefire talks. He then talks about Vice President JD Vance’s old supposed admiration for Bernie Sanders, pivots back to the ceasefire discussion, then to a Bollywood actress being attacked for simply following him, then back to shifting global interests in response to the US-Israeli war on Iran, and so on and so forth.

And in between, he keeps responding to comments, his tone oscillating constantly. Thousands of viewers are asking him questions, laughing at his jokes, offering their own forms of analysis, expressing gratitude for his thoughts, wishing each other good morning or good night, or simply opining as flippantly as Piker.

“‘Hey man, I just got here, can you start the stream over?’” one chatter says.

“Sure buddy, I got you,” Piker replies, flashing the waiting room graphic on the screen, and then re-fading it off. “What’s up everybody, I’m live and alive,” he says, with a chuckle. “Fucking idiot.”

Piker first began streaming in 2018, while he still worked at The Young Turks, the media company co-founded by his uncle, Cenk Uygur. His days were long. He’d start writing a morning breakdown for TYT starting at 5am. Then film it. Then jot down production notes and collect B-roll. He’d look at the first edit, and provide additional notes to the editing team. Then he’d reformat the whole thing as an article to publish on HuffPost. He’d come home and make dinner. Then he’d livestream, for four, five, or six hours.

By 2019, Piker was streaming even longer, spending much less time seeing friends or family. In 2020, he made the jump and began streaming fully independent. He would stream for 8-10 hours a day, every day.

His routine grew even more intense during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, which is also when he lost his earlier dog, Fish, to cancer. “It broke me,” Piker says. (He would not feel ready to go on to get Kaya until 2023.)

“I was all alone, lost my best friend, and I guess that’s also part of the reason why I just dove into Twitch streaming,” he continues. “I livestreamed every waking moment.” He livestreamed for 42% of the entire year, he estimates. (Now, it’s around 30%, or less, he adds.)

Many people during the pandemic were naturally looking for community.

It was during that time that many of Piker’s listeners found him. To this day, the most common thing he hears from people in public is that they first discovered him in 2020.

He tells me he didn’t regret how intense his schedule felt back then, even at its most consuming, because it was worth it. There’s, of course, the financial independence and freedom he’s been able to build. He does have a pricey house, something critics have tried to suggest is hypocritical for someone with his politics.

But, there was something bigger than the material success. “I have a massive community of like-minded people whose minds have changed throughout the years, who are very active in their own communities, and are fighting the good fight,” he says, whether they are active online, or organizing protests and rallies, and bringing more people into the fold. “I’ve got so many people in my community that have gotten their workplaces unionized. So I am eternally grateful for that.”

Now, Piker’s schedule has calcified into reading the news at 6am, taking Kaya to the dog park, exercising, returning home, freshening up, and streaming by around 11am.

Fitness has buoyed Piker through the demanding schedule. Accordingly, much of the public perception around him relates to his body. Article after article over the years have, ironically or not, highlighted his body, height, and physical appearance. “It’s not great,” he says when I ask about how central his body seems to how he is perceived by observers. His work may be in a visual medium, but his fitness routine is for him.

“I work out because it makes me feel better. I work out and want to have a healthy body because it helps me deal with everything else that I’m doing,” he says, explaining that the regimen helps him deal with the amount of streaming, and thus sitting, he does, and the gravity of the content he covers. “If I skip working out for a couple days, I start feeling like shit,” he explains. “I get sad, get a little depressed, so I definitely have to keep up with that.”

Piker started working out in earnest after college, when he moved to Los Angeles. His friends were personal trainers who were very keen with their diets, spurring him to find his discipline. And soon, the day arrived: He got his own six-pack.

Piker says the milestone instilled something new he’s carried beyond fitness. “It sounds silly, but it’s one of the most consequential things that I always wanted, and I could never achieve it,” he says. “And now that I have achieved it, I know that I have the power to achieve whatever I want to, as long as I work really hard, and I’m very stubborn, and push through all of the hurdles.”

As much as he is a political lightning rod, Piker is also an avatar for young people around the world. Especially when it comes to navigating online landmines surrounding fitness and body image.

Piker says he’s been subject to some of the same social forces perturbing young people today: pockets of the internet that push people towards eating disorders, and other online spaces that feed an “unhealthy obsession” with one’s looks. He notes it’s become “a universal experience all teenagers go through.”

Piker emphasizes that teenage girls were already dealing with these issues, but, thanks to the “manosphere,” boys now find themselves navigating similar problems.

It’s among the topics he addresses in his commentary, to encourage young people to have a healthier outlook not grounded solely in aesthetics.

“I’m on the other side of it now, so I don’t really care about it as much, but, back then, I think I was just young and insecure,” he says. “I still have body dysmorphia… I’m aware of it. So when I see myself, I know that the way I see myself is always far worse than how others perceive me.”

At one point in our conversation, as we discuss what Piker was like as a child, Piker’s mom, who was present within earshot at the time, chimes in.

“Very stylish,” she quips. She says Piker was very interested in drawing, seemingly an outlet for his energy. She brings out some of his old sketchbooks. “It wasn’t enough that he drew in his notebooks. He drew all over the desk as well… and he was super hyperactive,” she recalled.

We leaf through his old notebooks. Sketches of anime, comic book and video game characters, and body forms adorn the pages. As he looks back on the drawings, Piker recounts how he actually wanted to pursue art as a career.

“At school, he was a menace,” his mom says, joking: “The teachers would cry when they saw me.”

“He could question everything,” she adds. “That’s part of the problem.”

Instead of becoming an artist, Piker has built one of the largest online audiences in US politics, including the largest political audience on Twitch in the US. His audience is uniquely large for a leftist content creator. He also speaks to a younger audience. All of it makes him a threat to the political establishment, and it also means he speaks with less of a filter, more profanely, which leaves what he says more open to exploitation by his enemies.

Piker has shined a spotlight on Israel’s US-backed genocide in Palestine, railed against the ills of Western capitalism and corporate greed, and used his large platform to spread class consciousness and support leftist political candidates.

As a result, from Fox to the Democratic Party establishment, Piker has gone from an inconvenient thorn to a massive target.

For his critics on the right, like those at Fox, Piker’s response is simple and echoes FDR: “They’re united in their hatred of me and I welcome their hatred.”

“This is going to be corny to say, but: ‘Their boos mean nothing when I’ve seen what makes them cheer,’” he says. “That’s the Rick and Morty quote, I can’t believe I’m saying that, but it’s a bar and it fits so perfectly in this circumstance. I’m sorry. If you are championing Donald Trump and his Epstein buddies, the pedophilic rapist class of finance-capital overlords that have dominated American society unconditionally, you’ve defended a man who ran on Haitians eating cats and dogs, engaging in white vengeance, white nativist politics, insistently dividing the working class in this country and saying a great deal of heinous things, including, but not limited to, a couple of weeks ago, saying he’s going to do a genocide, he’s going to knock off the entire Iranian ‘civilization’ overnight. This person is a monster. If you’re defending that guy, I don’t care if you turn around and call me a radical. I don’t care if you turn around and say I’m an enemy – I am an enemy to you. You are my enemy.”

And for those in the center – like the centrist think tank Third Way, which has demanded that Democratic politicians condemn Piker – he shares similar sentiments, arguing that such groups “are not real.”

“They’re a group of consultants that just get paid by lifeless, faceless, parasitic capital [to] dig around and try to be a mediator, a middleman, between the interests of Exxon, Chevron, Aetna, Blue Cross Blue Shield, and Democratic Party electeds,” he says.

Recently, politicians and pundits from the far right and the center left have dredged up a litany of Piker’s most controversial comments, often lacking context, in order to try to discredit and smear him.

Perhaps the most weaponized comment is when he said that “America deserved 9/11.”

Piker was reacting to a 2019 clip of podcaster Joe Rogan speaking with Republican Rep. Dan Crenshaw. Rogan was asking Crenshaw whether US actions or its massive military presence around the world may foment terrorism. Crenshaw dismissed the notion. At one point – explaining why the US maintains presence in Syria, Iraq, and Afghanistan – he said “these people over there wake up every single day trying to plan another 9/11.” Throughout the discussion, Crenshaw suggested threats against the US were incited more by ideology, and hatred of the West, rather than US actions.

The US military presence in over 100 countries is because “we’re there to partner with them,” Crenshaw claimed.

“I just fucking snapped,” Piker tells me. “I was like, ‘What is this fucking mentality? Like, this is exactly why, you know, Americans deserved 9/11, we totally brought it upon ourselves.’ And that part never gets – I mean, that part is in the clips, but they don’t even understand what I’m saying at that point: We are responsible for 9/11, and we are still pursuing the same mentality even to this day. What I was referencing was blowback.”

He continues, “I don’t think people that bring it up as a real problem are doing so because they think I’m pro 9/11. Who the fuck is pro-9/11?” Piker poses. “They’re just saying that to be like, ‘This is a Muslim guy who loves terrorism. We got him. He proved it himself.’”

Piker has also been made out to be an antisemite. One common quote cited by his critics is Piker describing some Orthodox Jewish people in Israel as “inbred.”

He has expressed regret about the comment, but countered that he’s used the term as a pejorative against a wide array of groups, pointing to a compilation of him calling “calling every kind of reactionary, neo-Nazi, white supremacist Republicans, ‘inbreds.’”

“It’s vulgar. I know it’s vulgar. I want to be hurtful. I want to be mean to these people that are doing heinous crimes,” Piker tells me. He’s confounded by those who read the quote by itself, as if he was “randomly calling Jewish people inbreeders.”

“There’s a game of telephone with these quotes where it originates from me saying, ‘Look at these racist inbred ultra-Zionist settlers, destroying humanitarian aid at the Gaza border crossing,’ to ‘Hasan Piker called Orthodox Jews inbred,’ to ‘Hasan Piker calls all Jews inbred,’ and it’s not the case,” he says.

One of Piker’s fans created a 30-minute compilation of the streamer speaking against antisemitism. He has called antisemitism “a canary in the coal mine of fascism.” He’s said there’s “nothing grosser than both being antisemitic and then using the cause of Palestine to feed people more antisemitic commentary.” He’s repeatedly argued against conflating the government of Israel with Jewish people.

A newer line of attack against Piker has been the suggestion that he is illiberal, an avowed authoritarian. Piker has been accused of peddling pro-Communist propaganda during a recent visit to China, and has been criticized for saying, at a recent event at Yale University, that the fall of the Soviet Union was “one of the greatest catastrophes of the 20th century.”

For the former, Piker has acknowledged China’s censorship practices, noting how he could not do what he does in the US in China. But, he said, the hope of his visit was to “show that China isn’t this hermit kingdom the way it’s often presented,” and foster “a better understanding of other people.” As for the latter, Piker has argued that the fall of the USSR resulted in “incalculable” harm to the nations that were part of it, and that it ended a multipolar world, accelerating neoliberalism that is “devastating every Western nation.”

Piker has not stood by all his past remarks. In an old sketch video from 2013, he joked: “Come back again next week, when we discuss the legendary question of all time – old enough to count, old enough to mount?”

“It was a failed attempt at satire that was supposed to be like a bro show, and it was so cringe and so awful that I immediately deleted it,” Piker tells me.

He frames that moment as part of his journey. He notes he mostly had progressive opinions at the time, but was transphobic and misogynistic, too. “That is something that I have talked about openly for many years at this point,” Piker says. “I talk about privilege blind spots, and the intersectionality of privilege, and how we also have to be more graceful – and I know it’s difficult, especially if you’re a part of the vulnerable group that is being offended – but I talk about how we have to be a little bit more graceful and remind ourselves that everyone is on a journey and that we, as leftists, have to be more invested in rehabilitation.”

Piker is not just armchair quarterbacking. He has been active in supporting candidates like New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani. He recently mobilized voters at two rallies alongside progressive primary candidates such as Abdul El-Sayed, who’s running for Senate in Michigan. He’s platformed congressional candidates like Adam Hamawy in New Jersey – a doctor who served in Gaza and is credited with saving Illinois Senator Tammy Duckworth’s life during the Iraq War.

After Piker’s Michigan rallies were announced, El-Sayed’s opponents launched a smear campaign against him, with Michigan Senator Elissa Slotkin and candidates Haley Stevens and Mallory McMorrow loudly condemning Piker. The campaign did not work. Recent polling has shown Piker to have positive favorability ID in the state, albeit with little name recognition. And, after Piker appeared alongside El-Sayed, a poll showed El-Sayed surging in the primary.

Piker weathered similar failed attacks when he came out in support of Mamdani and had him on his show.

Outside of traditional party politics, Piker seeks to push people into organizations like the Democratic Socialists of America, where they can find an organizing community, and help show the country a different way of doing politics.

To him, Mamdani’s popularity illustrates this. “All of a sudden, people look at him and go, ‘Wait a minute. This guy says he’s a Democratic socialist. I have learned my whole life that socialism is a scary thing, but this is a handsome guy who smiles a lot, who’s fixing the potholes.’ That’s sewer socialism through and through,” he says.

Speaking more about Mamdani’s governance, Piker adds: “I think that is very helpful in advancing this cause and showing Americans that there is a way to do more responsive politics, a way to reinstill confidence in good governance. Because I think many Americans, understandably, are very resistant to that mentality that your government is supposed to help you when you need it, your government is supposed to fix certain things, because it hasn’t. So that’s what I’m trying to achieve, to let 1,000 Zohrans bloom.”

Of course, the Democratic Party establishment didn’t want the first Zohran to bloom. That has made Piker’s relationship “antagonistic at times, unnecessarily,” he says, because, in his view, he’s seeking to help the party.

“If this is supposed to be a more representative force of the needs of the masses, they should act like it, but they don’t, because they’re captured by corporate interests and that is my main issue with the Democratic Party,” he says.

Instead, to his critics, he’s an unproductive spoiler – “because I dare demand more,” as he puts it.

Piker did not endorse Kamala Harris in 2024 – a decision he still stands by. He tried to warn her campaign about the political risks posed by the Biden administration’s support for Israel’s genocide in Gaza, and her own campaign’s refusal to engage voters on the issue.

Beyond some sparse verbal overtures, the pleas were largely ignored. The Democratic National Convention – where Piker streamed from – famously refused to allow a Palestinian to speak on stage, further inflaming tensions within the party over Israel and Gaza.

Piker ultimately advised people to vote their conscience. He wasn’t going to castigate anyone for their vote, unless it was for Donald Trump.

On April 10, Piker attended a film screening in Los Angeles of ‘The Voice of Hind Rajab,’ a documentary about Israel murdering a 5-year-old girl, after killing her family members in front of her.

Piker addressed an audience of 100-some attendees, and discussed how the Democratic Party could be pushed “in the appropriate direction” despite some Democrats having participated “enthusiastically in the genocide of the Palestinians” up to this point.

He recalls how much he cried when he first saw the film. It was a “release” of the emotion he banked up from hours every day covering the thousands of kids just like Hind killed by Israeli forces. That coverage has also included interviewing a teenager live from Gaza who had been displaced three times within weeks amid Israeli bombardment, and amplifying doctors who served in Gaza, who recounted their experiences surviving Israeli violence and treating horrific injuries.

It’s this kind of horror that Piker, and millions of his viewers, have witnessed – and they’ve seen how many people in power have not only refused to contend with it, but actively supported it.

Today, Israel’s violence is not just a concern among the left. It is increasingly mainstream to oppose Israel’s genocidal violence in Palestine, and beyond, and to oppose unconditional US support for it. That is especially the case in the Democratic Party, where majorities now view Israel unfavorably.

Some on the right do, too. Two of the most prominent figures representing this shift are former far-right Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene and ex-Fox host Tucker Carlson, who agree with human rights experts and progressive leaders that Israel is committing genocide.

Their views have spurred talk of a potential left-right coalition – a conversation that has been stoked by Greene and progressive Rep. Ro Khanna, as well as Piker’s uncle Cenk Uygur and his colleague Ana Kasparian. Piker said he’s thrown out “feelers” about going on Carlson’s show but hasn’t heard back, and that he’d probably be willing to host Carlson on his show too.

Still, Piker finds the left-right coalition idea is “too media-brained.” Proponents are missing the numbers, Piker says. He notes Carlson’s approval plummeted on the right amid his public disagreements with Trump, and that Republican approval for military action in Iran shot up after Trump decided to launch his illegal war.

In any case, Piker has his eye on Carlson. “I think that he is establishing himself as a credible anti-war voice right now – and maybe it’s actually sincere, I’m even willing to entertain that. Outside of all the other heinous things that he believes, maybe this time he saw too many dead children and that changed his mind in some way on this issue,” Piker says.

Whether or not Carlson’s intentions are genuine, Piker adds, the former Fox host could use that “established credibility to either run” in 2028 – or “lend that credibility to a JD Vance-style figure.”

And if the Democrats run a “Gavin Newsom-type” against Carlson or whomever he blesses, Piker says, “They will destroy the Democratic Party, 100%, and we will deserve it.”

“Our inability to address this issue two election cycles in a row, our resistance to our own base’s desires, it will be devastating, and it will be entirely in the hands of the Democratic Party, just as last election’s defeat was,” he says.

As we sit in his office discussing the litany of attacks he’s weathered, Piked refers to a title bestowed on him by comedian Brennan Lee Mulligan.

Earlier this year, during a podcast discussion about Piker’s role in the media and streaming landscape, Mulligan referred to him as a “sin eater for the world.”

As Piker summarizes it, Mulligan described him as someone who “has to suffer for every leftist sin and any gripe that anyone has, any animosity they feel toward like what they perceive as woke or left or whatever. I am the human embodiment of that.”

But how, in a world filled with so much horror, has Piker come to fulfill this role? For whom is Piker the embodiment of sin?

In the US, there is an untenable status quo – one that the political and media class seeks to preserve. There is colossal and expanding wealth inequality, underscored by an increasingly visible crop of billionaires, and of sexual abusers infiltrating all facets of public life. Costs of living are prohibitive, and only getting worse thanks to the policy decisions of the people in charge – who are far more focused on funding genocide and foreign wars than attending to the needs of the people they represent.

As public opinion shifts, particularly on Israel, elites are looking to stop the bleeding. For them, Piker represents both a convenient target, based on his history of controversial remarks, and a necessary one.

Because it’s not his body, style, vulgarity, controversy, or methods alone that have brought Piker so central to the moment. It’s that he offers a place in which people can process the chaos all around them. He presents a mirror to the layperson – and a window to the world – so they can see they are not crazy. And he insists that, no matter how vulgar he is or is made to be, nothing can match the obscenity of Israel murdering a child, surrounded by the corpses of her family members, and the world answering with resounding silence.

For those who’ve helped create this world, it’s necessary that Hasan Piker eats sin. Because otherwise, he is an invitation for everyone else to understand whose sins they actually are.
