item.title: *2.45PM ET TODAY* Top Catholic Priest on the Pope vs Donald Trump and JD Vance
item.description: Jesuit priest James Martin has defended Pope Leo from Trump’s attacks and is challenging the weaponization of Christianity to justify war in Iran. He'll take your questions during a live Town Hall. Jesuit priest James Martin has defended Pope Leo from Trump’s attacks and is challenging the…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Jesuit priest James Martin has defended Pope Leo from Trump’s attacks and is challenging the weaponization of Christianity to justify war in Iran. He'll take your questions during a live Town Hall.

Jesuit priest James Martin has defended Pope Leo from Trump’s attacks and is challenging the weaponization of Christianity to justify war in Iran. He'll take your questions during a live Town Hall.

Photo illustration showing a picture of Donald Trump and an AI-generated image he posted online depicting himself as Jesus Christ. Photo by Mandel NGAN/AFP via Getty Images

President Donald Trump, Vice President JD Vance and other members of the administration have been engaged in a strange battle with Pope Leo XIV, sparked by the Pontiff’s stance against the war in Iran. It’s just the latest in a long streak of disrespect towards Christians and Christianity throughout Trump’s presidency. As administration officials defend the US strikes in Iran as a “just war,” Catholic leaders are rejecting this claim, saying that it does not fit the doctrine of just war established by the Church.

Rev. James Martin, a Jesuit priest, best-selling author on Catholicism, and editor at large of America Magazine, has warned about efforts by American politicians to “marginalize” the Pope’s voice and convince people to not listen to the Holy Father’s words denouncing warmongers.

Martin will join Mehdi in a live Zeteo town hall today, Tuesday, at 2:45pm ET (11:45am PT / 7:45pm GMT) to discuss the Trump administration’s attacks on the Pope, its weaponization of Christian beliefs, and whether there is any truth to the claims of a “just war.”

They’ll also discuss that now-notorious and deleted image that Trump posted portraying himself as Jesus Christ.

Attendees will be limited to paid Zeteo subscribers, who have the chance to ask their own questions to Martin as well. Register now below.

item.title: Trump Is Making Republicans Wet Their Beds
item.description: On this day in 1966, three activists staged a “Sip-In” in Greenwich Village in New York, demanding service from bars refusing to serve gay men. Predating the more famous Stonewall Riot by three years, the protest was nonetheless a key event in the movement for gay rights. Good morning. It’s Swin…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1966, three activists staged a “Sip-In” in Greenwich Village in New York, demanding service from bars refusing to serve gay men. Predating the more famous Stonewall Riot by three years, the protest was nonetheless a key event in the movement for gay rights.

Good morning. It’s Swin again. Today is another day ending in “Y,” so that means it’s another day of President Donald Trumptrying to rig the midterms. You should not be complacent at all about this authoritarian threat, but his corrupt efforts are probably going to fail. One of the key reasons for that is that this president is so towering in his unpopularity that it makes the 2026 elections even harder to steal than they otherwise would have been. It’s an acidic level of unpopularity that Trump’s party and administration reeeeealllllllly cannot spin away – and boy, is there panic starting to show in some darkly comic and desperate ways. More on that below…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Republicans know their policies are a political problem, Trump’s scandal-plagued labor secretary bites the dust, GOP lawmakers have a bill to denaturalize US citizens, and hopefully, soon you’ll have a good reason to read Infowars.

In the past few months, according to three sources with direct knowledge of this matter, officials in the Trump White House have privately begged a number of their hardcore, anti-immigration allies to lay off the president for the rest of the year. The White House’s secret message goes as follows: Please stop yelling at us for pausing certain tactics since the height of our Minnesota siege. Republicans need to win the midterms. Our mass deportation policies are clearly a drag on our poll numbers. We promise to ramp the ethnic cleansing back up to 11 after the midterms, okay?

I am, of course, paraphrasing. But the message was loud and clear: GOP leaders have seen all the numbers, and are queasy about how their own policy priorities might be doing them in, electorally speaking. “I’ve heard that argument a million times, and it’s generally accepted as the political posture of this administration – and it’s widely rejected by those of us in the Mass Deportation Coalition,” Mike Howell, a former Trump DHS official who is leading a new network dedicated to pressuring the Trump-Vance administration on this issue, tells me. “We were told not to talk about mass deportation before the midterms, so instead we are screaming it – across the country.”

To be fair to Howell’s coalition, they are only asking President Trump to keep doing what he promised the country, if not world, that he would do in a second term. But it’s not just Trump’s militarized ethnic cleansing that’s giving conservative politicians and consultants reason to fear the wrath of the midterms.

Ahead of Election Day in November, you could easily argue that the Republican Party’s greatest obstacle to keep the House and the Senate isn’t the woefully inept Democratic Party. It’s Trump’s (and the Republican Party’s) disastrous, illegal bloodbath in the Middle East, which is jacking up gas prices and shoving the United States – and planet – to the brink of complete economic rupture.

Since last year, we’ve been reporting on how behind the scenes, the national Republican elite have been freaking out that Trump’s stagnantly “ awful” poll numbers would invariably drag his party down in the 2026 elections. As of this month – per conversations I’ve had with an array of Trump appointees, longtime Republican operatives, conservative media luminaries, and GOP lawmakers – those anxieties have only skyrocketed at a higher altitude, and it’s in large part driven by the Iran war. They all say they’re fucked. They’re just trying to figure out just how badly. And yet, primarily out of fear of

“The bedwetting is making the whole bedroom damp,” a senior Trump administration official, speaking on the condition of anonymity (naturally), tells me.

They add that the intra-party freakout is now worse than what you see in public view, even. And boy, is what we see publicly really bad… if you’re a Republican.

With the president’s approval rating sliding deeper into the abyss, the Trump White House convened an emergency meeting at a luxury DC hotel with top GOP operatives in an effort to figure out how to staunch the bleeding. Fresh polling has come out showing that the majority of registered voters (correctly) blame the Republican president for spiking gas prices. GOP politicians and operatives in battleground states keep openly warning that the Senate is slipping out of their hands – something that wasn’t a pervasive fear all that many months ago. And top Republican senators can’t seem to stop begging this president – with tears in their eyes, as he would say – to pretty-please end his war, so that they don’t get electorally slaughtered come November.

“I hope that we are arriving at an exit strategy here to bring this to a close to preserve our security interests and bring down the cost of gasoline,” Senator Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) told the press in recent days, adding that the “ clock is ticking,” and speaking with all the confidence of a hostage under a searingly annoying level of duress.

In their hopes of forestalling a historic midterms implosion, the GOP elite is praying (sometimes literally) for cheaper gas. On Sunday, Trump’s energy secretary, Chris Wright, went on TV to say that Americans may have to expect gas to remain above $3 per gallon into next year.

In response to hearing about this, one of the GOP’s most prominent national operatives only had this to message me: “HOLY SHIT JUST SAY YOU LIKE LOSING.”

So things are going great.

Death tolls:

Iran: An Iranian official said this week that the death toll from US-Israeli strikes is at 3,375, including 383 children. It was unclear whether the death toll also included military and security forces.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,294 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Screed test: In a rant, Trump called Democratic critics of his war “weak and pathetic” and “traitors all,” and insisted the Iran war was “on the scale of Venezuela” but at the same time “just a bigger, more complex operation.”

Resume fire: Trump signaled that he will likely not extend the ceasefire set to expire on Wednesday, and threatened to drop bombs if no deal is reached. The president said he expected peace talks to continue, but said he was in no rush to end the conflict.

Threats threat: Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Ghalibaf said Tehran would refuse to engage in talks with the US if Trump continued making threats.

Force majeure: Kuwait said it could not fulfill obligations to deliver crude oil and refined products thanks to circumstances in the Strait of Hormuz.

Dire warnings: UN officials are sounding the alarm on the energy crisis due to the war in Iran. The conflict has triggered “the most severe energy crisis in a generation,” said Secretary-General António Guterres. Meanwhile, UN climate chief Simon Stiell said: “Fossil fuel-driven stagflation is now stalking economies, driving up prices, driving down growth, pushing budgets deeper into the quagmire of debt and stripping away governments’ policy options and autonomy.”

Lebanon latest: Lebanese media reported that Israel was continuing its strikes on villages along the border, despite the 10-day ceasefire in place. Meanwhile, Spain, Slovenia, and Ireland urged the European Union to debate suspending its association agreement with Israel over its actions in Gaza, the West Bank, and Lebanon.

For months, I’ve wanted to write a piece for Zeteo explaining how world-historically wild it is that the Trump-Vance administration is so corrupt, so violently authoritarian, so scandal-plagued that you simply did not know what is going on at the Department of Labor right now. (Ok, maybe some of you did.) But President Trump won’t stop invading and bombing places, so I’ve been busy and never wrote it. So I’ll make the point here.

On Monday, NOTUS broke the news that Trump’s labor secretary, Lori Chavez-DeRemer, had finally resigned.

It was about damn time. During any other administration in modern US history, the things that occurred at the highest levels of her Labor Department would have dominated cable news. The tumult at the Scott Pruitt-era EPA certainly drove TV news cycles during Trump 1.0, and yet all of that now looks downright quaint compared to the depraved-reality-TV-style flood of scandal and alleged misconduct that came out of Chavez-DeRemer’s Labor Department. Here’s a sample of headlines from recent months:

People: “Labor Secretary’s Husband and Father Sent Personal Texts to Her Young Female Staffers, Report Claims: ‘Please Keep This Private’”

NYT: “Labor Secretary’s Husband Barred From the Department After Sexual Assault Reports”

CBS: “Labor secretary’s husband won’t face charges after being banned from Labor Dept. building, sources say”

Politico: “DC police searched Labor Secretary’s office in sexual assault investigation”

New York Post: “Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer’s security guard resigns amid probe into allegations of ‘inappropriate’ relationship: sources”

Independent: “Trump’s labor secretary accused of taking staff to a strip club on official trip, report says”

Salem Statesman Journal: “Lori Chavez-DeRemer reportedly under investigation for misusing funds”

Chavez-DeRemer’s story was buried under the full tonnage of media coverage devoted to the numerous other scandals, abuses, wars, killing sprees, cover-ups, rantings and ravings, and eruptions of all-American fascism that the second Trump administration produces on an hourly basis.

After Chavez-DeRemer resigned Monday, White House communications director Steven Cheung said she did “a phenomenal job.”

Keep in mind: Chavez-DeRemer was supposed to be the normal one when Trump was putting together his cabinet of debauched clowns and madmen. Clearly, she was never an outlier.

Suits you: FBI Director Kash Patel filed a $250 million defamation lawsuit against The Atlantic for its report about allegations of excessive drinking, erratic behavior, and absences from work, which Patel denies. The Atlantic plans to fight the suit.

Class Onion: The Onion announced its takeover of Infowars, long run by on-off Trump ally and Sandy Hook denier Alex Jones. The satirical title aims to relaunch the conspiracist website as a parody of itself.

Union dues: In a blow for centrist Democrats, the Colorado AFL-CIO endorsed Julie Gonzales, a progressive challenger to sitting US senator John Hickenlooper.

Acting up: Far-right Texas GOP congressman Chip Roy introduced the MAMDANI Act, an Islamophobic measure named after the New York mayor and containing “Measures Against Marxism’s Dangerous Adherents and Noxious Islamists,” including deportation and denaturalization.

Cleaning House: After the exits of Democrat Eric Swalwell and Republican Tony Gonzales, the House Ethics Committee said it would maintain a “workplace free from” sexual misconduct. Republican Cory Mills still faces such allegations. Nancy Mace filed a motion to expel, calling for “moral courage to do what’s right.”

Profit alert: The far-right influencer and Holocaust denier Nick Fuentes has made $900,000 from donors since last year, the Washington Post reported, detailing giving by people with little money of their own, including through paid messages or “superchats.”

For which old-time practice did Texas Republican Chip Roy appear to advocate in a 2021 US House hearing about anti-Asian hate, earning widespread rebuke?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Jesuit priest James Martin will join Mehdi in a live Zeteo town hall TODAY at 2:45pm ET (11:45am PT / 7:45pm GMT) to discuss the Trump administration’s attacks on the pope, its weaponization of Christian beliefs, the blasphemous AI image of the president as Jesus, and whether there is any truth to the claims of a “just war” against Iran.

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: The EU, UN, and World Bank estimated that reconstruction and recovery will require $71.4 billion over a decade.

🇵🇸 Deadly day in West Bank: Israeli settlers killed two Palestinians, including a 13-year-old boy, and injured several others during an attack on a school in the village of al-Mughayyir, east of Ramallah, Palestinian media reported. Separately, a car tied to an Israeli minister’s security detail hit and killed a 16-year-old Palestinian on a bike, Haaretz reported.

🇰🇼 Reporter held: Today marks 50 days in detention in Kuwait for Kuwaiti-American journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin, arrested after sharing previously published pictures of a crashed US plane. Calls for his release continue.

🇸🇩 Switching sides: Maj. Gen. al-Nour Ahmed Adam, a top Rapid Support Forces (RSF) commander, left the group to join Sudan’s national army, four years after the civil war began, the Sudan Tribune said.

🇺🇬🇨🇬 Captives out: Ugandan and Congolese forces freed more than 200 civilians from Allied Democratic Forces in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo.


The GOP is the party of xenophobia and white supremacy. And they’re not even ashamed of it. This racist, anti-Black congressman, by the way, is married to the daughter of… Indian immigrants.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: Trump Is Making Republicans Wet Their Beds
item.description: On this day in 1966, three activists staged a “Sip-In” in Greenwich Village in New York, demanding service from bars refusing to serve gay men. Predating the more famous Stonewall Riot by three years, the protest was nonetheless a key event in the movement for gay rights. Good morning. It’s Swin…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1966, three activists staged a “Sip-In” in Greenwich Village in New York, demanding service from bars refusing to serve gay men. Predating the more famous Stonewall Riot by three years, the protest was nonetheless a key event in the movement for gay rights.

Good morning. It’s Swin again. Today is another day ending in “Y,” so that means it’s another day of President Donald Trumptrying to rig the midterms. You should not be complacent at all about this authoritarian threat, but his corrupt efforts are probably going to fail. One of the key reasons for that is that this president is so towering in his unpopularity that it makes the 2026 elections even harder to steal than they otherwise would have been. It’s an acidic level of unpopularity that Trump’s party and administration reeeeealllllllly cannot spin away – and boy, is there panic starting to show in some darkly comic and desperate ways. More on that below…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Republicans know their policies are a political problem, Trump’s scandal-plagued labor secretary bites the dust, GOP lawmakers have a bill to denaturalize US citizens, and hopefully, soon you’ll have a good reason to read Infowars.

In the past few months, according to three sources with direct knowledge of this matter, officials in the Trump White House have privately begged a number of their hardcore, anti-immigration allies to lay off the president for the rest of the year. The White House’s secret message goes as follows: Please stop yelling at us for pausing certain tactics since the height of our Minnesota siege. Republicans need to win the midterms. Our mass deportation policies are clearly a drag on our poll numbers. We promise to ramp the ethnic cleansing back up to 11 after the midterms, okay?

I am, of course, paraphrasing. But the message was loud and clear: GOP leaders have seen all the numbers, and are queasy about how their own policy priorities might be doing them in, electorally speaking. “I’ve heard that argument a million times, and it’s generally accepted as the political posture of this administration – and it’s widely rejected by those of us in the Mass Deportation Coalition,” Mike Howell, a former Trump DHS official who is leading a new network dedicated to pressuring the Trump-Vance administration on this issue, tells me. “We were told not to talk about mass deportation before the midterms, so instead we are screaming it – across the country.”

To be fair to Howell’s coalition, they are only asking President Trump to keep doing what he promised the country, if not world, that he would do in a second term. But it’s not just Trump’s militarized ethnic cleansing that’s giving conservative politicians and consultants reason to fear the wrath of the midterms.

Ahead of Election Day in November, you could easily argue that the Republican Party’s greatest obstacle to keep the House and the Senate isn’t the woefully inept Democratic Party. It’s Trump’s (and the Republican Party’s) disastrous, illegal bloodbath in the Middle East, which is jacking up gas prices and shoving the United States – and planet – to the brink of complete economic rupture.

Since last year, we’ve been reporting on how behind the scenes, the national Republican elite have been freaking out that Trump’s stagnantly “ awful” poll numbers would invariably drag his party down in the 2026 elections. As of this month – per conversations I’ve had with an array of Trump appointees, longtime Republican operatives, conservative media luminaries, and GOP lawmakers – those anxieties have only skyrocketed at a higher altitude, and it’s in large part driven by the Iran war. They all say they’re fucked. They’re just trying to figure out just how badly. And yet, primarily out of fear of

In response to hearing about this, one of the GOP’s most prominent national operatives only had this to message me: “HOLY SHIT JUST SAY YOU LIKE LOSING.”

So things are going great.

Iran: An Iranian official said this week that the death toll from US-Israeli strikes is at 3,375, including 383 children. It was unclear whether the death toll also included military and security forces.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,294 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

item.title: Big Tech Is Powering Israel's AI War Machine
item.description: Israel’s genocide in Gaza exposed how AI is changing warfare in terrifying and deadly ways. With the help of Big Tech, Israel, along with the US, is now expanding its use of AI in Iran and Lebanon, widening its testing ground for AI-enabled military technology – with more deadly consequences.…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Israel’s genocide in Gaza exposed how AI is changing warfare in terrifying and deadly ways. With the help of Big Tech, Israel, along with the US, is now expanding its use of AI in Iran and Lebanon, widening its testing ground for AI-enabled military technology – with more deadly consequences.

Israel has long been one of the world’s leading hubs for AI development. As of 2024, there were about 2,300 AI startups active in Israel, according to a report by Google Israel and RISE Israel, a technology think tank. Since then, that number has likely only risen as venture capitalists pour money into the industry. Roughly one quarter of all technology startups in Israel are AI companies, supported by billions of dollars in funding.

Tech giants have also deepened their relationships with Israel, tapping into their tech ecosystem to recruit talent and grow their businesses. Nvidia recently announced plans to build a massive tech campus in northern Israel that will be home to AI research and development projects, and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has described Israel as the company’s “second home.”

All of this has happened as Israel assaults civilian populations in Gaza, the West Bank, Lebanon, and Iran.

A new report from the International Institute for Strategic Studies (ISS), a think tank and leading authority on global security and military conflict, details exactly how heavily integrated AI is in Israel’s weapons systems and military operations.

According to the report, Israel has made significant investments to embed AI across its military tools and systems. These efforts range from the creation of an AI and Autonomy Administration within the Ministry of Defense’s Directorate of Defense Research & Development, to empowering the elite intelligence Unit 8200 to build out the Israeli military’s own AI capabilities.

AI is being used to improve target tracking and kill rates of anyone Israel deems to be enemy combatants. Israeli company Smart Shooter, for example, has developed an AI-powered “smart” weapon sight that can be installed on almost any firearm. Once appended to the gun, it can transform a standard rifle into a far more deadly precision-guided firearm.

Thanks to AI technology, a shooter can peer at a target using Smart Shooter’s SMASH optic system, and the software will lock onto the target and automatically track the person as they move. When the shooter goes to pull the trigger, the system automatically synchronizes the shot release as soon as the weapon is aimed properly at the target. Basically, the trigger is electronically blocked until the AI system calculates that the shot will actually hit, making each shot far more likely to be deadly. Smart Shooter’s SMASH system can also be used on drones.

One of Israel’s most depraved AI products is known as Lavender. Throughout the genocide in Gaza, Israel has leveraged Lavender, an AI tool that aggregates large volumes of data on people and applies pattern-recognition algorithms to identify which people to kill, often tracking them and killing them at their homes along with their families, leading to maximum casualties.

Using this system, the Israeli army has marked tens of thousands of Palestinians as potential targets for assassination, according to +972 Magazine, and its AI targeting system has almost no human oversight and seems to be extremely permissive when it comes to casualties.

“According to six Israeli intelligence officers, who have all served in the army during the current war on the Gaza Strip and had first-hand involvement with the use of AI to generate targets for assassination, Lavender has played a central role in the unprecedented bombing of Palestinians, especially during the early stages of the war,” +972 reported. “In fact, according to the sources, its influence on the military’s operations was such that they essentially treated the outputs of the AI machine “as if it were a human decision.”

The ISS report also highlights Israel’s use of AI facial recognition systems. Israel has rolled out sprawling facial-recognition programs in both Gaza and the West Bank. In the West Bank, the Israeli military uses systems that access a database called Wolf Pack. The database stores highly sensitive personal information on Palestinians, which is used to create intelligence profiles that are then shared with Israel’s internal security agency, Shin Bet.

Palestinian’s biometric data is harvested at checkpoints throughout Israel, and Israeli soldiers have Wolf Pack-affiliated software installed on their smartphones in order to feed more Palestinians into their systems. Running facial-recognition programs on people without their consent on such a massive scale is strictly against international human rights laws. The right to privacy, including against facial recognition systems, is guaranteed under Article 17 of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR).

None of these AI surveillance or AI weapons systems could function without support from the commercial market. American defense tech and AI companies like Palantir profit directly from their contracts with the Israeli military. In 2024, Israel and Palantir signed a strategic partnership to “harness Palantir’s advanced technology in support of war-related missions.”

But also, “big-tech companies such as Amazon, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI have provided AI products to a variety of defence ministries, including the US and Israel,” the ISS report notes. “A 2024 draft contract between Google and the Israeli defence ministry highlighted the latter’s pre-existing exclusive ‘landing zone’ to access cloud infrastructure, and new plans to create specific landing zones for military units.”

The report also reveals that under the terms of Project Nimbus, Israel’s state-owned Israel Aerospace Industries and Rafael Defense Industries are required to use Google and Amazon’s cloud services for all cloud-computing needs. “As Israel stands accused of war crimes, crimes against humanity and genocide at both the International Criminal Court and the International Court of Justice, its deepening cooperation with commercial providers could potentially expose them to liability under both domestic and international-law frameworks,” ISS adds.

While America’s two largest AI giants, Anthropic and OpenAI, haven’t formally partnered with the Israeli military directly, they’re already deeply intertwined with its military actions. The Israeli military has had access to OpenAI’s GPT-4 language model via its partnership with Microsoft since August 2023. The LLM is capable of helping the military analyze billions of pieces of information and synthesize data. (Last year, Microsoft reportedly revoked Israel’s elite intel Unit 8200 from accessing the technology, but it continues to work with other units of the Israeli military, per +972.)

While OpenAI’s terms initially banned the use of its services for “military and warfare” activities, the company quietly removed this clause and expanded its partnership with the US military in January 2024, amidst the escalating assault on Gaza.

Anthropic has sought to restrict use of its models by the US military in certain ways; however, Claude, Anthropic’s primary model, is embedded in Palantir’s systems and was reportedly used by the US military to identify targets in Iran. The ISS report notes that “AI-enabled military technologies can be expected to continue to proliferate across Middle Eastern battlefields, scaling up the damage done to civilians and civilian objects and exacerbating humanitarian crises.”

As Big Tech companies deepen their ties with the US military and continue doing business with the Israeli military, the Middle East will remain a laboratory for AI-enabled warfare, and the innocent civilians in Gaza, the West Bank, Lebanon, and Iran will pay the price for Silicon Valley’s latest AI experiments.

Taylor Lorenz, author of the Zeteo column, ‘Network Effect,’ is an acclaimed tech and online culture journalist. For more of Taylor’s writing, subscribe to her Substack, User Mag.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: ‘Blame the British Empire’: Mehdi and Prem on Iran, Palestine, and Kashmir
item.description: As US-Israeli violence escalates across Iran, Lebanon, and Palestine, the region’s future becomes increasingly uncertain. In Palestine, the question remains: what’s the path forward? Will it look like South Africa? India-Pakistan? “The British government in 1947 and 1948 created the mess in Kashmir…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
As US-Israeli violence escalates across Iran, Lebanon, and Palestine, the region’s future becomes increasingly uncertain. In Palestine, the question remains: what’s the path forward? Will it look like South Africa? India-Pakistan?

“The British government in 1947 and 1948 created the mess in Kashmir with India-Pakistan, created the mess with Palestine with the mandate… A lot to answer for, my home country,” says Mehdi as he answers a subscriber’s question on ‘Ask the Editor.’

‘Ask the Editor’ is Zeteo’s weekly Q&A show where subscribers can tune in live on Zeteo.com and YouTube. Watch the full video above to hear Prem and Mehdi also unpack the following:

Is the ceasefire in Iran breaking down?

Who is actually qualified to negotiate with Iran? JD Vance? Marco Rubio?

The words the mainstream media uses to “sane-wash” Donald Trump

How the pro-Israel lobby became a “global phenomenon”

Why do Democrats always “have just enough votes to lose” key progressive votes in Congress?

…and, as Mehdi neatly puts it: “Why do we live in the dumbest timeline in the world where the guy from ‘Home Alone 2’ has mounted a possibly armageddon-starting war that didn’t need to be fought on behalf of an indicted war criminal from Tel Aviv, and now they don’t know how to end the war because they’re too stupid, they’re too dumb, they’re too stubborn, they’re too violent?”

Let us know what you think in the comments, and subscribe to catch our next episode of Ask the Editor every Monday at 11am ET on Zeteo.com, YouTube, or on the Substack app.

If you believe in the work we do and want to see more of it, do consider becoming a paid subscriber. A donation to Zeteo also goes a long way.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: The ‘Peace President’ Threatens an Apocalypse on Iran. AGAIN
item.description: On this day in 1979, President Jimmy Carter was photographed fending off an angry rabbit while fishing from a boat on a Georgia pond. The story was widely seen to have contributed to Carter’s defeat by Ronald Reagan in the 1980 election. Hello, readers – it’s Swin again. Some of you have no doubt…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1979, President Jimmy Carter was photographed fending off an angry rabbit while fishing from a boat on a Georgia pond. The story was widely seen to have contributed to Carter’s defeat by Ronald Reagan in the 1980 election.

Hello, readers – it’s Swin again. Some of you have no doubt grown accustomed to my incessant ‘Simpsons’ references in this newsletter, seeing as how quoting clips from that show is one of the few ways I know how to adequately convey the sheer stupidity of the Trump-Vance era. This morning, I have another for you: President Donald Trump’s negotiation skills during this phase of his illegal war with Iran have been on par with the pre-hockey game deal Chief Wiggum strikes with the criminals at Springfield’s jail. But as this past weekend showed, on top of the self-defeating tactics and bumbling own goals, you also get this president’s mentally desiccated eruptions of insatiable bloodlust.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Trump issues more genocidal threats while oil prices rise and his approval rating craters, and, in Michigan, a centrist Democrat backed by AIPAC and eyeing a seat in the US Senate finds support in short supply.

Nearly two weeks ago, Mehdi and I were on a Zeteo livestream, offering our live reactions to Donald Trump’s just announced decision to back off from his own deadline for his own war, for which the self-professed “PEACE PRESIDENT” had vowed an explicitly genocidal, “civilization”-destroying blitz of war crimes on the Iranian people, if they didn’t give what he demanded. Trump, thankfully, blinked.

A hyper-tenuous two-week ceasefire (if you can call it that while keeping a straight face) would emerge. Still, on that livestream, I issued a warning to our viewers: That same night, Trump administration officials, citing recent conversations with the president, were stressing to us in real time that the chances that Trump would escalate the war anyways were still extremely high. At the end of the stream, Mehdi asked the panel if we’d be back in two weeks, talking about how everything went to shit. I wagered less than two weeks, hoping I’d be wrong.

It’s been less than two weeks, and we’re coming off of a weekend where the former host of NBC’s ‘Celebrity Apprentice’ somehow turned president of the United States is not only back to his schtick of casually threatening an apocalyptic barrage of infrastructure-destroying attacks and mass murder, but he’s once again sharply escalating his war of aggression in the Middle East.

“My Representatives are going to Islamabad, Pakistan – They will be there tomorrow evening, for Negotiations … We’re offering a very fair and reasonable DEAL, and I hope they take it because, if they don’t, the United States is going to knock out every single Power Plant, and every single Bridge, in Iran. NO MORE MR. NICE GUY,” Trump posted Sunday morning.

Later, he announced that US armed forces had, in yet another dramatic escalation, attacked and seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship near the Strait of Hormuz, as the Americans and Iranians continued their dueling blockades. Iran’s government has effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz since the start of the war, choking off the flow of oil and sending energy prices soaring, throttling the global economy.

Two senior US officials and two Trump advisers I spoke to this weekend all expected the war to significantly intensify very soon.

As he continues threatening textbook examples of blatant war crimes, Trump is at least publicly claiming that he’s optimistic about the latest round of supposed peace talks in Pakistan. Unsurprisingly, the Iranians signaled this week that they very much are not and that they consider these peace talks to be a farce. An Iranian official said earlier today that while no final decision had been made, Tehran so far had no plans to attend any upcoming talks in Islamabad.

At this time, all sides of this conflict look like they’re slamming clenched fists on the Escalation Button – with President Trump inviting even more economic destruction and political suicide. The world desperately needs the Strait of Hormuz open; in recent days, the price of oil shot down on the global market (though not at the US pump yet) because people were expecting (hoping?) this war would wrap up soon. Within the coming days, oil prices will likely spike again, along with the killing, and the Iranian regime is once again threatening to impose greater pain should Trump not officially cry uncle.

Technically, Trump had a way out of this… instead, the war could keep spiraling out of control, in an election year, to an extent where $4 gas might not seem that bad!

Whatever happens in the near term, we as a nation are at a juncture in modern history where it is commonplace, if not wholly expected, for our genocidal president – in our name, funded by our hard-earned tax money – to dangle horrific, “civilization”-ending war crimes on civilian targets any time he feels like it… and for our GOP-controlled Congress to just let him do it whenever he wants.

This is a type of uniquely pathetic barbarism that we as Americans want to believe we only read about, in chapters of world history like the Albigensian Crusade. And yet it’s routinely coming from the words and actions of our so-called leader of the free world.

We haven’t even begun to feel the long-term impacts of what Trump and his enablers and allies have done. We will continue reporting on this aggressively here at Zeteo and especially ‘First Draft’ in the coming days and weeks. Things are relentlessly bleak, but, hey… at least we don’t have to worry about Donald Trump invading Cuba soon, right?

Death tolls:

Iran: An Iranian official said today that the death toll from US-Israeli strikes is at 3,375, including 383 children. It was unclear whether the death toll also included military and security forces.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,294 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Ship seized: The US said it hit and seized an Iranian cargo ship as part of its blockade of Iranian ports, prompting Tehran to promise retaliation, two days shy of the scheduled end of the ceasefire.

Trump gloats: In a post, the US president said the ship had been sanctioned since 2019 and added that US Marines were “seeing what’s on board.”

Barreling on: Oil prices rose, heading back towards $100 a barrel, after news of renewed tensions over the Strait of Hormuz.

Talks fear: European countries worry that US negotiators only care about a quick win for Trump, Reuters reported, adding that diplomats think the American rush to secure a deal with Iran will only guarantee years of complex talks to come.

Christ statue smashed: The Israeli military said a picture of one of its soldiers destroying a statue of Christ in southern Lebanon was real, adding that it “views the incident with great severity” and will take “appropriate measures” against those involved.

Donald Trump has reached new depths of unpopularity, according to an NBC News poll, with just 37% of Americans approving of his second-term performance and 50% of those who disapproved saying they did so strongly. A full two-thirds of respondents said they disapproved of Trump’s handling of inflation and the conflict with Iran. One area where those two issues meet is at the gas pump: Two-thirds of Americans answering the survey said gas prices were a problem for them and their family. Trump was even down four points with Republicans, though 83% still backed him.

According to Politico, GOP unease is spreading to operatives in battleground states, who are beginning to voice real concern that Trump’s unpopularity could see the Senate, and not just the House, fall to the Democrats in November.

Too true: US Ambassador to the UN Mike Waltz made an apparent Freudian slip, referring to Trump’s attack on Iran, Operation Epic Fury, as “Operation Economic Fury.”

Boos news: Michigan US Senate hopeful and AIPAC favorite Representative Haley Stevens was loudly booed as she spoke at the state Democratic convention.

Screens test: The Strokes ended their set at Coachella by displaying protest messages highlighting US-backed coups, killings, and interventions, including the war in Iran and support for Israel’s attacks on Gaza.

Kash dash: FBI director Kash Patel said yesterday that a lawsuit against The Atlantic over its report about his alleged excessive drinking and unexplained absences was “coming tomorrow.” The Atlantic editor Jeffrey Goldberg said he stood by the story.

ICE watch: A federal judge in Long Island described “police state” tactics employed by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), including arresting people and drawing up warrants after the fact.

Court scoop: The New York Times published secret memos that showed how the Supreme Court in 2016 created the “shadow docket,” the controversial mechanism that has created rulings in favor of the Trump administration.

Another progressive wins: Defeating the incumbent, Amir Makled, a lawyer who represented University of Michigan student protesters, won the Democratic nomination for the school’s governing board during the party’s state endorsement convention this weekend.

Which Premier League football team does FBI director Kash Patel appear to support, judging by his controversial neckwear choices for visits to Congress?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Is the US violating the ‘ceasefire’ with Iran?

Join Mehdi and Prem TODAY at 11am ET, 8am PT, or 4pm GMT, for Zeteo’s ‘Ask the Editor.’

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Hamas officials say they’re willing to turn over some weapons to the Palestinian administrative committee, according to the New York Times. Meanwhile, Haaretz reported that Palestinians in Gaza say they’re having to stand in increasingly long lines to wait for bread as flour and fuel shortages worsen amid Israel’s continued restrictions on aid entering the Strip.

🇬🇧 🇵🇸 Spying on students: Al Jazeera reported today that 12 universities in the UK paid hundreds of thousands of dollars to a private firm to “spy” on student protesters and academics, including Palestine supporters and animal rights activists.

🇧🇬 Electoral shift: The center-left Progressive Bulgaria party appeared to win Bulgaria’s election, according to exit polls. It beat the center-right GERB party, which has long dominated, but a coalition government will need to be formed.

🇨🇩 Temporary truce: The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and the M23 rebel group took first steps towards a long-term peace deal, agreeing to allow aid deliveries, release prisoners, and stop targeting civilians.

🇲🇾 Devastating fire: A fire spread through a village in Malaysia’s Sabah state, destroying roughly 1,000 homes and displacing thousands.


In 2018, as you can see in this video that re-emerged over the weekend and went viral online, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer blamed Palestinians for there being “no peace in the Middle East” and invoked the Torah.

In 2026, Schumer continues to refuse to hold Israel accountable for war crimes and acts of ethnic cleansing, being one of seven Democrats to vote last week against blocking weapons to Israel. As Mehdi argued in Friday’s ‘First Draft,’ Senate Democrats need to do the right thing – and replace him immediately.

Abdul El-Sayed’s support is up 8 points in the latest Emerson poll, and he is now tied with Mallory McMorrow. I guess campaigning with Hasan Piker really hurt him, right? RIGHT??

Mike Waltz told ABC’s ‘This Week’ that “all options are on the table” in Iran, including destroying power plants and bridges, and rejected the idea that attacks on civilian infrastructure are war crimes.

The GOP is completely allergic to facts. When CNN’s Jake Tapper pressed Rep. Elise Stefanik on Trump’s call to wipe out the “whole civilization” of Iran, Stefanik refused to call the comments genocidal language. “President Trump didn’t call for genocide, Jake. You are putting those words in his mouth,” she said.

The administration is seemingly escalating its efforts to claim the 2020 elections were rigged. FBI Director Kash Patel, a long-time election denier, told Fox’s Maria Bartiromo he has “all the information” he needs to make arrests soon.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright walked back claims that inflated gas prices would only last for “weeks,” not “months,” telling CNN he has no idea when prices will go below $3. “That could happen later this year,” Wright said. “That might happen until next year. But prices have likely peaked.”

The solution to gerrymandering is AI – or so GOP Rep. Nicole Maliotakis proposed on CNN’s ‘State of the Union’ as a way to produce fair districts, ahead of the Virginia redistricting referendum.

Monday, April 20: Elon Musk is set to be questioned by French cybercrime prosecutors in their ongoing investigation of X. Plus, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer is expected to address the House of Commons regarding his appointment of Epstein-affiliate Peter Mandelson as ambassador to the US. And the Supreme Court is scheduled to hear oral arguments in Sripetch v. SEC, which could strip the SEC of its regulatory powers.

Tuesday, April 21: Virginians are set to vote on a new electoral map in which Democrats could win up to 10 House seats. Plus, US judges will hear arguments over the Justice Department’s efforts to force New Mexico to hand over voter data.

Wednesday, April 22: California gubernatorial candidates Chad Bianco, Katie Porter, Tom Steyer, and Steve Hilton will participate in a debate on NewsNation.

Saturday, April 25: Trump is set to headline “the most exclusive crypto & business conference” at Mar-a-Lago for the top 297 holders of his $TRUMP crypto memecoin. He’s also expected to attend the White House Correspondents’ Dinner for the first time as president.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: What's Really Happening Between Trump and Iran? Mehdi Takes Your Questions LIVE on Monday
item.description: Did the US just violate the Iran ceasefire? In the wake of the US reportedly firing at and seizing an Iranian-flagged cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, join Mehdi and Prem to break down this escalation, and all the latest from this war. Tune into ‘Ask the Editor:’ a live Q&A on Monday at 11am…
item.author: Zeteo

Did the US just violate the Iran ceasefire?

In the wake of the US reportedly firing at and seizing an Iranian-flagged cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, join Mehdi and Prem to break down this escalation, and all the latest from this war.

Tune into ‘Ask the Editor:’ a live Q&A on Monday at 11am ET, 8am PT, or 4pm GMT. Zeteo subscribers can join the conversation on Zeteo.com, the Substack app, or YouTube.
