item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1865, Abraham Lincoln was shot dead by John Wilkes Booth at Ford’s Theatre in Washington, DC. Three more presidents – James A Garfield, William McKinley, and John F Kennedy – have been assassinated. Donald Trump is one of 13 presidents who have survived assassination attempts.

Good morning. It’s Swin and Andrew here. In the second Trump era, we realize how difficult it can be – even for avid, astute news consumers – to keep track of all the madness. For instance, the president invaded Venezuela this year, then immediately pivoted to threatening an invasion of Greenland, only to move on to further invading parts of his own country, right before starting a new, illegal bloodbath in the Middle East.

Donald Trump isn’t even done with his failing war on Iran yet – but he could soon add another country to the list.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the president has his team planning another act of war elsewhere, two accused creeps in Congress are calling it quits, and the Republican Party is a big tent when it comes to whether its politicians believe the US president should proclaim himself Jesus and scream at the pope.

Kidnapper Plots

History’s cattiest warmonger, Donald Trump, continues to publicly dangle a US invasion of Cuba, the island nation that his administration has been strangling to death with a brutal, months-long energy blockade.

“We may stop by Cuba after we’re finished with this,” Trump said Monday – meaning once his disastrous war in Iran actually ends.

It was an insane moment, as Trump stood next to a woman wearing a “DoorDash grandma” shirt for a corporate White House photo-op. (At the same event, Trump claimed he had only mistakenly committed blasphemy when he posted an image depicting himself as Jesus, because he thought it was “me as a doctor.”)

This isn’t the first time the president has threatened to invade Cuba. It’s not even the first time he’s made this threat during the US-Israeli war in Iran, which is throttling the global economy. “Cuba is going to be next,” Trump said two weeks ago.

It would be one thing if President Trump were only mouthing off for the cameras. He’s not.

In recent days, according to two sources familiar with the situation and another person briefed on it, officials at the Pentagon and elsewhere in the US government were quietly given a new directive that came straight from the Trump White House. The message: ramp up your preparations for possible military operations against Cuba.

It appears that Trump has not made a final decision yet on military action, and negotiations to end Trump’s “economic bombing” of the island’s civilian population are ongoing. If Trump doesn’t get what he wants, Zeteo is told that one option that has been discussed within the Trump administration is for the US military to conduct another abduction operation against some of the Cuban leadership, similar to its kidnapping of Nicolás Maduro in Venezuela early this year.

The Pentagon referred questions to the White House. Asked for comment, a White House official tells Zeteo: “As the president stated, we are talking to Cuba, whose leaders want to make a deal and should make a deal, which President Trump believes ‘would be very easily made.’ Cuba is a failing nation whose rulers have had a major setback with the loss of support from Venezuela.”

Trump’s administration appears to be laying the groundwork to run its same Maduro playbook in Cuba. Last month, the New York Times reported that the Trump Justice Department is leading “a broad-ranging inquiry into Cuba’s leaders and Communist Party officials for drug, immigration, economic, and violent crimes with a goal of bringing fast indictments.”

The US president, two of the sources tell us, has grown increasingly frustrated lately with the open defiance from the Cuban government, including its president, Miguel Díaz-Canel, who recently said: “You hear that Cuba is next, that Cuba is going to be next, that there’s a way out, that they’re going to take over Cuba… We need to responsibly protect our people, protect our project, and protect our country.”

One Trump adviser compared Trump’s frustration to the annoyance he felt at seeing video of Maduro dancing, before he had him kidnapped and brought to the US.

It is objectively disturbing that we can write these words about the nominal leader of the free world, and not only does it seem routine at this point in the Trump era; it would almost be weird if this particular government wasn’t plotting these things today.

Donald Trump has been rampaging across the globe, in some of the most barbaric and heinous ways, for more than a year now. Hyper-violent imperialism isn’t a bug in the MAGA agenda; it is perhaps its defining, most disgraceful feature – and we will be living with the horrifying consequences of Trump’s pathetic rampage for years if not decades to come.

Trump’s potential warmongering pivot to Cuba comes as President Donald Trump’s six-week-old war with Iran continues to spin out of control, creating a mammoth political disaster for him at home and a regional bloodbath abroad.

The Trump-Vance administration, however, does not appear to be done with its MAGAfied warmongering.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s Human Rights Activists News Agency said last week the death toll from US-Israeli strikes stands at more than 3,000, including 1,700 civilians.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,089 people, including at least 165 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Blockade begins: Trump said the US military had blocked all Iranian ports in an effort to force the re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz. On Truth Social, Trump wrote that if any Iranian ships came close to the blockade, they would be “immediately ELIMINATED.”

Nuclear suspension: Trump rejected an offer from Iran to suspend uranium enrichment for five years as he seeks to push his original proposal of a 20-year suspension.

Pontiff tiff: Trump said he would not apologize to Leo XIV for his attacks over the pope’s criticism of the Iran war, or for posting a picture of himself as Jesus. “I think he’s very weak on crime and other things,” Trump said.

Marching orders: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu told a cabinet meeting Vice President JD Vance “reported to me in detail” from his flight home from failed peace talks in Pakistan, saying, “in this case, the explosion came from the American side.”

Hezbollah opposed: A senior official said the Iran-backed militant group would not abide by any results of Israel-Lebanon talks due to be held in the US.

Not automatic: Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni has reportedly said her government is suspending the auto-renewal of a defense agreement with Israel “in light of the current situation.” An Italian defense official told Reuters that as part of the suspension, Italy will halt some cooperation with Israel on military training.

GOP to Trump: ‘Christ on a bike!’

Republicans were more outspoken than usual in the face of a Trump outrage, after the president took his clashes with Pope Leo XIV over his war in Iran to bizarre lengths, posting a picture of himself as Jesus, then claiming, amid general opprobrium, to have taken it down only because he’d thought it was a picture of him as a doctor.

Utah Senator John Curtis found Trump’s post “offensive,” adding (somewhat contentiously for a modern-day Republican): “We have a really healthy relationship in the United States between religion and government, and there should be respect.” Senator Susan Collins of Maine was characteristically concerned. More interestingly, Senate Majority Leader John Thune said, “I would leave the church alone.”

There was also support for Trump. Vice President JD Vance, an ardent Catholic convert, said his boss posted a joke but took it down “because people weren’t understanding his humor in that case,” while also advising the pope to “stick to matters of morality.” Ohio Senator Bernie Moreno echoed Vance, saying it was “a shame the Pope has made the Catholic Church political” and adding, bizarrely, “Thank God my mom’s not alive to watch that.” South Carolina Senator Lindsey Graham, who cheerleaded Trump into war in Iran in tandem with Israeli leader Benjamin Netanyahu, tried to speak directly to Leo. “You’re miscalculating here, pope,” he said.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Prize fight: Trump is reportedly due to present an award to the Wall Street Journal – for reporting on his bawdy birthday letter to Jeffrey Epstein, over which he sued and lost.

Leaving in disgrace: Democrat Eric Swalwell said he would quit the US House over allegations of sexual assault, which he denies, a day after exiting the California governor’s race. Tony Gonzales, a Texas Republican accused of sexual misconduct, said he would retire on Tuesday.

Leak speak: In a court filing, the Pentagon admitted it changed its media policy in the aim of reducing mainstream stories based on sensitive information.

ICE probe: A Minnesota county is investigating the ICE arrest of an elderly Hmong American man as a potential case of kidnapping, burglary, and false imprisonment.

Amazon death: When a worker died suddenly in an Amazon warehouse in Oregon, employees were told to look away and keep working, the Western Edge news outlet said, in just the latest report of alleged harsh practices at the tech giant.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

On Monday, Donald Trump took down a Truth Social post showing him as Jesus but left up an image showing him as which other major religious figure?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Another former aid group contractor told Middle East Eye and Drop Site that he saw Israeli troops using deadly force against unarmed aid seekers. “He was doing nothing but laughing and joking and they killed him,” David McIntosh said, referring to a young boy he says an Israeli sniper shot as the child played near an aid site last year. “There’s no way he survived.”

🇸🇸 Town capture: The Sudan People’s Liberation Army-in-Opposition announced that it had captured the strategic town of Akobo in South Sudan’s Jonglei state, after government troops seized it back in March

🇨🇦 Carney victory: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney gained a Liberal majority government after his party won all three seats in Monday’s parliamentary special elections, effectively giving a green light to pass his legislative agenda with few roadblocks.

🇵🇪 Election disruption: Voting in Peru was extended into Monday amid widespread delays at polling locations during Sunday’s general election. Conservative Keiko Fujimori leads in preliminary presidential results.

📊 Chart of the Day

The share of US consumers saying they feel worse off than they did a year ago is now 54%, the highest mark ever recorded. How about that fizzing Trump economy, eh?

🙄 WTF?!

The president cannot simply function as a normal person around ordinary people.

🧠 Trivia answer: The pope.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
21 hours later, US-Iran ceasefire talks crashed and failed, courtesy of the dream team sent to seal the deal: “Loser-in-Chief” JD Vance, Steve Witkoff, and, of course, noted “failed property developer” and “Israeli asset” Jared Kushner.

“This is a government filled with inept, incompetent people,”says Mehdi to Prem as they break down what went wrong on ‘Ask the Editor’ – Zeteo’s weekly live show where subscribers can ask questions on YouTube and Substack.

Watch the full video above to hear Prem, Mehdi, and Zeteo subscribers unpack:

Whether the US can actually blockade Iran or the Strait of Hormuz

How Team Trump is feeding Benjamin Netanyahu “daily updates”

Whether pro-Palestine voters should or should not support Tucker Carlson in a supposed presidential campaign

Donald Trump’s irreligious posing as Jesus on Truth Social

…and much more!

Let us know what you think in the comments, and subscribe to catch our next episode of Ask the Editor every Monday at 11am ET on Zeteo.com, YouTube, or on the Substack app.

If you believe in the work we do and want to see more of it, do consider becoming a paid subscriber. A donation to Zeteo also goes a long way.

item.title: 9 Times Trump Has Ridiculously Insulted Christians
item.description: Donald Trump may not be a church-going man, but he sure loves to invoke God when it suits his wallet or his ego. From picking fights with the Pope to profiting off the Bible instead of reading it, Trump has turned faith into farce for his own benefit. The MAGA cult has remained bizarrely loyal to…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump may not be a church-going man, but he sure loves to invoke God when it suits his wallet or his ego.

From picking fights with the Pope to profiting off the Bible instead of reading it, Trump has turned faith into farce for his own benefit. The MAGA cult has remained bizarrely loyal to its “savior” of choice – even as he readily admits he is unlikely to make it into heaven.

Here are nine times Trump has insulted God and the Christians who elected him:

1. Depicting Himself as Jesus

On Sunday, the president of the United States posted an AI-generated photo that appeared to depict himself as Jesus.

He has since taken down the post, which drew rare ire from some religious figures on the right. When Trump was asked about the photo on Monday, he admitted to posting it himself but claimed he thought it was “me as a doctor” and it “had to do with Red Cross.” See it for yourself…

Ex-Trump ally and former conservative Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene screenshotted the post and wrote on Twitter: “It’s more than blasphemy. It’s an Antichrist spirit.”

2. Fighting With the Pope

The bizarre Jesus image came just after Trump dunked on Pope Leo XIV, calling him “WEAK on crime,” and “terrible for Foreign Policy.”

“Leo should get his act together as Pope, use Common Sense, stop catering to the Radical Left, and focus on being a Great Pope, not a Politician,” Trump raged on.

Later on video, Trump doubled down: “I’m not a big fan of Pope Leo. He’s a very liberal person, and he’s a man that doesn’t believe in stopping crime.”

The Pope, for his part, responded that he has “no fear of the Trump administration.” Imagine if Joe Biden or any other president, had picked a fight, publicly, with the Pope.

3. ‘I’d Like to Be Pope’

Maybe Trump is just mad that Pope Leo got the job he wanted.

Last year, following the death of Pope Francis, Trump posted an AI-generated image depicting himself as the new Pope.

“I’d like to be Pope,” Trump told reporters, adding that he would be his own “number-one choice” to serve as the next Pope.

4. ‘Second Coming of God’

In 2019, Trump publicly thanked right-wing radio host and conspiracy theorist Wayne Allyn Root for offering “very nice words” and calling him the “greatest President for Jews and for Israel in the history of the world.”

“The Jewish people in Israel love him like he’s the King of Israel,” Root said. “They love him like he is the second coming of God.”

The king of Israel? Perhaps “puppet” would be more appropriate.

5. Comparison to Jesus

During a White House Easter event, Trump thanked televangelist Paula White after she bizarrely compared the president to Jesus.

“You were betrayed and arrested and falsely accused. It’s a familiar pattern that our Lord and Savior showed us,” said White, senior advisor to the White House faith office. “But it didn’t end there for him, and it didn’t end there for you.”

Trump, of course, wasn’t falsely accused – he was indicted, both federally and in Georgia, over his very public efforts to overturn the results of an election he lost.

6. Favorite Bible Verse

When Bloomberg asked Trump in 2015 to share his favorite verse from the Bible, which Trump claims is his “favorite book,” the president declined to answer.

“I wouldn’t want to get into it because to me that’s very personal… The Bible means a lot to me but I won’t want to get into specifics,” Trump said.

Doesn’t “want to,” or can’t because he doesn’t read it?

7. Misquoting the Bible

At a 2016 speech at Virginia’s evangelical Liberty University, Trump botched a scripture reference as he tried to appease the church-going crowd.

“We’re going to protect Christianity. I can say that. I don’t have to be politically correct,” he said. “Two Corinthians, 3:17, that’s the whole ballgame … is that the one you like?”

Some in the audience quickly corrected him, saying it’s “Second Corinthians,” not “Two.”

8. Did Not Touch the Bible

At the inauguration last year, Trump did not touch the Bible when he took his oath of office, which may have been for the best.

When Trump was asked a few months later whether he had an obligation to uphold the Constitution, per his oath of office, Trump responded: “I don’t know.”

9. Selling Signed Bibles

Trump may not read the Bible but, ever the salesman, he will find a way to profit off of it.

He reaped $1.3 million in 2024 by endorsing “God Bless the USA” Bibles. These Bibles sell for $60 – and a version with Trump’s signature has sold for $1,000. Maybe the president should keep a copy and read it himself.

Zeteo’s Minnah Arshad contributed to this story.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: JD Vance Can't Stop Losing
item.description: On this day in 1873, an estimated 150 people were killed in Colfax, Louisiana, in the deadliest act of violence in the Reconstruction era. All but three of the dead were Black. The state made no arrests, and though 97 white men were arrested by federal authorities, the US Supreme Court ultimately…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1873, an estimated 150 people were killed in Colfax, Louisiana, in the deadliest act of violence in the Reconstruction era. All but three of the dead were Black. The state made no arrests, and though 97 white men were arrested by federal authorities, the US Supreme Court ultimately set them free.

Hi, all, it’s Swin here. Last week, on the same night President Donald Trump announced he would deign not to commit genocide just yet against Iran, I was on Zeteo’s livestream relaying a warning we were receiving in real time from sources inside the Trump-Vance administration: This isn’t over – not by a long shot. Trump’s supposed ceasefire was built on sand, if that, and the chances of the war escalating soon anyways were still high. It’s been less than a week since that night… and it looks like we are starting this week in the shadow of the weekend’s failed peace negotiations.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Trump screws over JD Vance; disgraced California congressman Eric Swalwell ends his gubernatorial campaign; AOC is “hustling,” and Graham Platner thinks Chuck Schumer will come around. Plus, we have the latest from the Middle East.

‘Imploding’ Trump Screws Over Vance

In late 2024, in the weeks directly following Donald Trump’s reelection, I had numerous conversations with incoming Trump administration officials and political operatives close to JD Vance, during which I used the same two-word phrase over and over: “Boy Kamala.”

My point (or, prediction, in some cases) was simple: There was little reason to believe Team Trump actually had a credible plan to bring down prices or inflation by any meaningful degree. Americans would continue to feel, and vote, like the US economy is a disaster, and they’d predictably go from blaming Joe Biden, Kamala Harris, and Democrats to punishing Trump, Vance, and the conservative ruling party. Vance’s future political, or presidential, aspirations were tied inextricably to how voters were going to feel about Trump, and Trumpism, by 2027.

There were multiple Vance compatriots who got weirdly mad or snitty at me for saying what I thought was a commonsensical point. Biden’s presidency sank Harris 2024. Unless Trump had an economic miracle shoved in his back pocket, it stood to reason that a similar fate would befall MAGA’s crown prince.

That was almost a year and a half ago… and even I could not have predicted then how utterly catastrophic things would get for Vance before 2026 was even half over.

To get the standard throat-clearing out of the way: Anything could happen between now and 2028. Nobody should count Vance out of any possible presidential contest. But as things currently stand, it is hard to imagine things going worse for the man President Trump has called his “handsome son of a bitch.” And it’s almost entirely because Trump forced Vance into this position.

“Trump is imploding, and he’s probably going to take JD down with him unless there’s a course correction,” one source close to Trump told me.

This weekend, the vice president and Trump-hater-turned-MAGA-diehard returned to Washington from Islamabad empty-handed. Trump sent Vance on a mission to secure a deal that would end Trump’s disastrous, illegal war in Iran – a war that Vance was reportedly squeamish about from the jump and that numerous members of Team Trump believe is tearing apart the administration in harrowing fashion.

The Iranians sent him packing.

Meanwhile, President Trump – as the Iranian government continued beating him at his own war – flailed in the direction of escalating the war once again, announcing the United States would blockade the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has closed the crucial waterway since the start of the war, cutting off the flow of oil and throttling the global economy. Trump’s latest idea, naturally, could very easily make the situation, somehow, worse.

And as the ceasefire talks fell apart, Trump sat ringside at a UFC fight in Miami, surrounded by the august likes of Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Vanilla Ice.

It gets worse for JD. Since last year, Vance has become the face of the Trump administration’s support for Hungary’s Viktor Orbán. The administration’s desire to prop up the European nation’s longtime authoritarian leader is a cornerstone of Team Trump’s efforts to create a vast international network of far-right populist parties. This was such a top priority for the Trump White House that they recently sent Vance to Hungary to campaign for Orbán. On Sunday, Orbán was dealt a crushing electoral defeat – meaning Vance’s losing streak stretched from Iran all the way to Central Europe, in just one weekend… though you could argue his losing streak began last April, when Vance met Pope Francis, who promptly died.

Domestically and electorally, the outlook isn’t any rosier for the vice president.

Last week, CNN’s chief data analyst Harry Enten crunched the numbers and informed viewers that ever since January 2025, Vance’s net approval rating has all but collapsed, with a 21-point swing downwards. “This might be part of a longer trend of vice presidents becoming unpopular, but we can say JD Vance is historically the least popular vice president at this point in their vice presidency,” the CNN analyst stressed.

At the end of the day, Americans are still suffering under Trump and his party’s violent authoritarianism, and people in multiple countries are dying by the thousands in a war Trump started and that Vance publicly cheered on, however mousily. The American vice president’s sagging luck does not change that.

But for now, Vance’s prospects are dimming at a time when (as Zeteo previously reported) his boss has snottily gossiped behind his back that the veep isn’t sufficiently enthused about the Iran war. Vance’s misfortunes are also spiking at a moment when the Trump administration is engulfed in a sprawling, paranoia-drenched behind-the-scenes leak hunt, after the president demanded to know which Cabinet members or senior administration officials blabbed some of his most closely held secrets (including on his Iran war) to reporters.

Vance – who in a past life compared Trump to Adolf Hitler (he didn’t mean it as a compliment at the time) – bet his entire political future on the success of Trump 2.0. But now, Trump 2.0 is in the process of eating him alive.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s Human Rights Activists News Agency said last week the death toll from US-Israeli strikes stands at more than 3,000, including 1,700 civilians.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,055 people, including at least 165 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 33 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Next step: US Central Command announced its naval blockade of Iran, beginning on Monday morning. It said it would not “impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports.” Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the UK was “not supporting” the blockade.

No surprise: Oil prices rose, with Brent crude oil surging 7% to above $100. Iranian Speaker Mohammed Bagher Ghalibaf posted a graphic of gas prices near the White House and said, “Soon you’ll be nostalgic for $4–$5 gas.”

No retreat: Trump seemed unconcerned about the prices, telling Fox that gas prices might go higher by the midterms. He also stood by his threat to commit war crimes.

Speaker vow: Ghalibaf said Iran would meet US actions in kind, saying: “If you fight, we will fight, and if you come forward with logic, we will deal with logic.”

Israel threat: In southern Lebanon, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu reiterated, “There is no ceasefire.”

Platner Wants Party’s Support

Oysterman Graham Platner doesn’t have support from Democratic Party leaders in Washington. He hopes that’ll change if he wins the primary. Zeteo’s Andrew Perez has the story:

At Maine’s first Senate debate on Saturday, Graham Platner reiterated that, if he wins, he would not support Chuck Schumer – who’s backing Janet Mills in the Democratic primary – staying on as the party’s leader in the Senate.

“We need to be getting people into places of leadership who have, I would say, probably a deeper structural critique of our politics, both our political system and our system at large, as to how we got here,” said Platner, who is leading Governor Mills in the polls. (Mills did not participate in the debate.)

Speaking with Zeteo, Platner said his campaign still hadn’t heard from Schumer and Senate Democratic leaders, but he expects to have the national party’s support if he wins the primary.

“If we win the primary, I have no doubt we’ll get that support,” he said. “Beating Susan Collins is the priority. There is no road to a blue Senate without going through Maine. And if they didn’t support us, I feel like there would be like a literal rebellion within the Senate, because people want to be in the majority more than they don’t like me. I would assume – at least, I hope so.”

Asked if he wants the national party’s support, Platner said, “Yeah, I would. I would assume we will [get it]. And if we don’t, that’s OK, because what we’ve built, we built it on our own anyway.”

Thank you, Andrew, who’s based in Maine. Keep following his reporting on the ground as Platner, and the rest of the state, approach their moment of truth.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Trump hates the pope: “Pope Leo is WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy,” Donald Trump posted Sunday evening, adding: “I don’t want a Pope who thinks it’s OK for Iran to have a Nuclear Weapon.” He doubled down on video: “I'm not a big fan of Pope Leo. He's a very liberal person, and he's a man that doesn't believe in stopping crime.”

Pope claps back: The pope later told reporters, “I have no fear of the Trump administration, or speaking out loudly of the message of the Gospel, which is what I believe I am here to do.” Asked what he thought about Trump’s comments on Truth Social, Leo added: “It’s ironic — the name of the site itself. Say no more.”

Swalwell out: Democrat Eric Swalwell ended his California gubernatorial campaign over sexual misconduct claims he denies. He faces calls to quit the US House, including from more than 50 ex-staffers. An expulsion vote could be next.

AOC TBC: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez is “hustling” to win over left-wing critics as she considers a run for either the US Senate or the presidency, Axios reported.

Poll plunge: A CBS and YouGov poll put Trump on a new low for his second term, at just 39% approval. He was also underwater on immigration (-12), Iran (-28, a new low), the economy (-30), and inflation (-38, another new low).

Judges out: The DOJ fired two immigration judges who dismissed high-profile immigration cases against Rümeysa Öztürk and Mohsen Mahdawi, foreign students arrested because they advocated for Palestinian rights.

Crypto row: Trump’s SEC settled a fraud case against Chinese crypto billionaire Justin Sun after he poured $75 million into the First Family’s crypto exchange, World Liberty Financial. Now, Sun says he’s a “victim,” and the company froze his crypto wallet. The firm responded, “See you in court, pal.”

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Donald Trump’s approval rating is at a new low, but it’s not as low as the lowest ever recorded by Gallup, 22%. Which president owns that dubious honor?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🚨 Don’t Miss It!

Is the Iran ceasefire over? Join Mehdi and Prem TODAY at 11am ET, 8am PT, or 4pm GMT, for Zeteo’s ‘Ask the Editor.’

💬 Quote Unquote

“Yes, antisemitism often cloaks itself in anti-Zionism. So don’t do the antisemites’ work for them. If you keep telling people that if they oppose the Jewish state then they must hate the Jewish people, eventually, they will believe you.”

Ezra Klein, in theNew York Times, on why leftist streamer Hasan Piker “isn’t a ‘Jew hater.’ He’s an anti-Zionist.”

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israeli airstrikes in Khan Younis and northern Gaza killed two Palestinians and injured several others, the Wafa agency said. Middle East Eye found an “organized state policy” of sexual violence against Palestinians in Israeli prisons.

🇭🇺 Orbán out: Far-right Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán was beaten after 16 years in power by Péter Magyar. Magyar’s center-right opposition party, Tisza, was on track to secure a two-thirds majority.

🇳🇬 Village attack: More than 100 people were killed and 35 others were injured in Yobe state as the Nigerian military launched airstrikes on Boko Haram militants, Amnesty said.

🇭🇹 Deadly rush: A stampede broke out at “La Citadelle,” a tourist attraction in northern Haiti, during an annual celebration. At least 30 people were killed.

🙄 WTF?!

Last night, after slamming the Pope, the president of the United States posted this AI-generated image, portraying himself as Jesus, to his Truth Social account. I am trying to imagine the screams of ‘sacrilege’ and ‘blasphemy’ from the right, especially from Christian evangelicals, if Barack Obama or Joe Biden had ever posted such an image of themselves.

🧠 Trivia answer: Harry S. Truman, in February 1952.

📺 Sunday Show Roundup

GOP Rep. Mike Turner claimed Trump’s message to Iran was clear. But when CBS’s Margaret Brennan confronted him with Trump’s statements on the Strait of Hormuz, showing how he flip-flops every couple of days, Turner could not give a straight answer on what Trump’s strategy is to reopen the strait. Turner also failed to respond to whether Congress has proper oversight of the Iran war.

Ahead of Pam Bondi’s scheduled deposition to discuss the Epstein files – which the DOJ says she will not attend – GOP Rep. Byron Donalds said on NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ that the former attorney general shouldn’t have to testify and he would not call for her to be held in contempt – even though he voted to hold Bill and Hillary Clinton in contempt for failing to comply with subpoenas of their own.

Some Democrats want Eric Swalwell to leave Congress, including Rep. Pramila Jayapal, who told NBC’s Kristen Welker she would vote to expel Swalwell, along with GOP Texas Rep. Tony Gonzales, who also faces misconduct allegations, if the House holds a vote on the issue.

Israeli officials are continuing to gaslight the world to rationalize war crimes against Lebanon. “We have no beef with Lebanon. On the contrary, we want to see a thriving Lebanon,” Israeli UN Ambassador Danny Danon told Fox.

🗓️ Mark Your Calendars

Monday, April 13: The heads of the International Energy Agency, International Monetary Fund, and World Bank are set to discuss the energy crisis.

Tuesday, April 14: Bondi is scheduled to appear before the House Oversight Committee for a deposition on her alleged mishandling of the Epstein investigation. The DOJ says she won’t attend.

Wednesday, April 15: White House Budget Director Russell Vought is expected to testify before the House Budget Committee regarding Trump’s 2027 budget request.

Thursday, April 16: NRA annual meetings are set to begin.

Friday, April 17: Trump is set to speak at a Turning Point USA event in Phoenix.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: JD Vance Can't Stop Losing
item.description: On this day in 1873, an estimated 150 people were killed in Colfax, Louisiana, in the deadliest act of violence in the Reconstruction era. All but three of the dead were Black. The state made no arrests, and though 97 white men were arrested by federal authorities, the US Supreme Court ultimately…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1873, an estimated 150 people were killed in Colfax, Louisiana, in the deadliest act of violence in the Reconstruction era. All but three of the dead were Black. The state made no arrests, and though 97 white men were arrested by federal authorities, the US Supreme Court ultimately set them free.

Hi, all, it’s Swin here. Last week, on the same night President Donald Trump announced he would deign not to commit genocide just yet against Iran, I was on Zeteo’s livestream relaying a warning we were receiving in real time from sources inside the Trump-Vance administration: This isn’t over – not by a long shot. Trump’s supposed ceasefire was built on sand, if that, and the chances of the war escalating soon anyways were still high. It’s been less than a week since that night… and it looks like we are starting this week in the shadow of the weekend’s failed peace negotiations.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Trump screws over JD Vance; disgraced California congressman Eric Swalwell ends his gubernatorial campaign; AOC is “hustling,” and Graham Platner thinks Chuck Schumer will come around. Plus, we have the latest from the Middle East.

‘Imploding’ Trump Screws Over Vance

In late 2024, in the weeks directly following Donald Trump’s reelection, I had numerous conversations with incoming Trump administration officials and political operatives close to JD Vance, during which I used the same two-word phrase over and over: “Boy Kamala.”

My point (or, prediction, in some cases) was simple: There was little reason to believe Team Trump actually had a credible plan to bring down prices or inflation by any meaningful degree. Americans would continue to feel, and vote, like the US economy is a disaster, and they’d predictably go from blaming Joe Biden, Kamala Harris, and Democrats to punishing Trump, Vance, and the conservative ruling party. Vance’s future political, or presidential, aspirations were tied inextricably to how voters were going to feel about Trump, and Trumpism, by 2027.

There were multiple Vance compatriots who got weirdly mad or snitty at me for saying what I thought was a commonsensical point. Biden’s presidency sank Harris 2024. Unless Trump had an economic miracle shoved in his back pocket, it stood to reason that a similar fate would befall MAGA’s crown prince.

That was almost a year and a half ago… and even I could not have predicted then how utterly catastrophic things would get for Vance before 2026 was even half over.

To get the standard throat-clearing out of the way: Anything could happen between now and 2028. Nobody should count Vance out of any possible presidential contest. But as things currently stand, it is hard to imagine things going worse for the man President Trump has called his “handsome son of a bitch.” And it’s almost entirely because Trump forced Vance into this position.

“Trump is imploding, and he’s probably going to take JD down with him unless there’s a course correction,” one source close to Trump told me.

This weekend, the vice president and Trump-hater-turned-MAGA-diehard returned to Washington from Islamabad empty-handed. Trump sent Vance on a mission to secure a deal that would end Trump’s disastrous, illegal war in Iran – a war that Vance was reportedly squeamish about from the jump and that numerous members of Team Trump believe is tearing apart the administration in harrowing fashion.

The Iranians sent him packing.

Meanwhile, President Trump – as the Iranian government continued beating him at his own war – flailed in the direction of escalating the war once again, announcing the United States would blockade the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has closed the crucial waterway since the start of the war, cutting off the flow of oil and throttling the global economy. Trump’s latest idea, naturally, could very easily make the situation, somehow, worse.

And as the ceasefire talks fell apart, Trump sat ringside at a UFC fight in Miami, surrounded by the august likes of Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Vanilla Ice.

It gets worse for JD. Since last year, Vance has become the face of the Trump administration’s support for Hungary’s Viktor Orbán. The administration’s desire to prop up the European nation’s longtime authoritarian leader is a cornerstone of Team Trump’s efforts to create a vast international network of far-right populist parties. This was such a top priority for the Trump White House that they recently sent Vance to Hungary to campaign for Orbán. On Sunday, Orbán was dealt a crushing electoral defeat – meaning Vance’s losing streak stretched from Iran all the way to Central Europe, in just one weekend… though you could argue his losing streak began last April, when Vance met Pope Francis, who promptly died.

Domestically and electorally, the outlook isn’t any rosier for the vice president.

Last week, CNN’s chief data analyst Harry Enten crunched the numbers and informed viewers that ever since January 2025, Vance’s net approval rating has all but collapsed, with a 21-point swing downwards. “This might be part of a longer trend of vice presidents becoming unpopular, but we can say JD Vance is historically the least popular vice president at this point in their vice presidency,” the CNN analyst stressed.

At the end of the day, Americans are still suffering under Trump and his party’s violent authoritarianism, and people in multiple countries are dying by the thousands in a war Trump started and that Vance publicly cheered on, however mousily. The American vice president’s sagging luck does not change that.

But for now, Vance’s prospects are dimming at a time when (as Zeteo previously reported) his boss has snottily gossiped behind his back that the veep isn’t sufficiently enthused about the Iran war. Vance’s misfortunes are also spiking at a moment when the Trump administration is engulfed in a sprawling, paranoia-drenched behind-the-scenes leak hunt, after the president demanded to know which Cabinet members or senior administration officials blabbed some of his most closely held secrets (including on his Iran war) to reporters.

Vance – who in a past life compared Trump to Adolf Hitler (he didn’t mean it as a compliment at the time) – bet his entire political future on the success of Trump 2.0. But now, Trump 2.0 is in the process of eating him alive.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s Human Rights Activists News Agency said last week the death toll from US-Israeli strikes stands at more than 3,000, including 1,700 civilians.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,055 people, including at least 165 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 33 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Next step: US Central Command announced its naval blockade of Iran, beginning on Monday morning. It said it would not “impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports.” Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the UK was “not supporting” the blockade.

No surprise: Oil prices rose, with Brent crude oil surging 7% to above $100. Iranian Speaker Mohammed Bagher Ghalibaf posted a graphic of gas prices near the White House and said, “Soon you’ll be nostalgic for $4–$5 gas.”

No retreat: Trump seemed unconcerned about the prices, telling Fox that gas prices might go higher by the midterms. He also stood by his threat to commit war crimes.

Speaker vow: Ghalibaf said Iran would meet US actions in kind, saying: “If you fight, we will fight, and if you come forward with logic, we will deal with logic.”

Israel threat: In southern Lebanon, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu reiterated, “There is no ceasefire.”

Platner Wants Party’s Support

Oysterman Graham Platner doesn’t have support from Democratic Party leaders in Washington. He hopes that’ll change if he wins the primary. Zeteo’s Andrew Perez has the story:

At Maine’s first Senate debate on Saturday, Graham Platner reiterated that, if he wins, he would not support Chuck Schumer – who’s backing Janet Mills in the Democratic primary – staying on as the party’s leader in the Senate.

“We need to be getting people into places of leadership who have, I would say, probably a deeper structural critique of our politics, both our political system and our system at large, as to how we got here,” said Platner, who is leading Governor Mills in the polls. (Mills did not participate in the debate.)

Speaking with Zeteo, Platner said his campaign still hadn’t heard from Schumer and Senate Democratic leaders, but he expects to have the national party’s support if he wins the primary.

“If we win the primary, I have no doubt we’ll get that support,” he said. “Beating Susan Collins is the priority. There is no road to a blue Senate without going through Maine. And if they didn’t support us, I feel like there would be like a literal rebellion within the Senate, because people want to be in the majority more than they don’t like me. I would assume – at least, I hope so.”

Asked if he wants the national party’s support, Platner said, “Yeah, I would. I would assume we will [get it]. And if we don’t, that’s OK, because what we’ve built, we built it on our own anyway.”

Thank you, Andrew, who’s based in Maine. Keep following his reporting on the ground as Platner, and the rest of the state, approach their moment of truth.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Trump hates the pope: “Pope Leo is WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy,” Donald Trump posted Sunday evening, adding: “I don’t want a Pope who thinks it’s OK for Iran to have a Nuclear Weapon.” He doubled down on video: “I'm not a big fan of Pope Leo. He's a very liberal person, and he's a man that doesn't believe in stopping crime.”

Pope claps back: The pope later told reporters, “I have no fear of the Trump administration, or speaking out loudly of the message of the Gospel, which is what I believe I am here to do.” Asked what he thought about Trump’s comments on Truth Social, Leo added: “It’s ironic — the name of the site itself. Say no more.”

Swalwell out: Democrat Eric Swalwell ended his California gubernatorial campaign over sexual misconduct claims he denies. He faces calls to quit the US House, including from more than 50 ex-staffers. An expulsion vote could be next.

AOC TBC: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez is “hustling” to win over left-wing critics as she considers a run for either the US Senate or the presidency, Axios reported.

Poll plunge: A CBS and YouGov poll put Trump on a new low for his second term, at just 39% approval. He was also underwater on immigration (-12), Iran (-28, a new low), the economy (-30), and inflation (-38, another new low).

Judges out: The DOJ fired two immigration judges who dismissed high-profile immigration cases against Rümeysa Öztürk and Mohsen Mahdawi, foreign students arrested because they advocated for Palestinian rights.

Crypto row: Trump’s SEC settled a fraud case against Chinese crypto billionaire Justin Sun after he poured $75 million into the First Family’s crypto exchange, World Liberty Financial. Now, Sun says he’s a “victim,” and the company froze his crypto wallet. The firm responded, “See you in court, pal.”

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Donald Trump’s approval rating is at a new low, but it’s not as low as the lowest ever recorded by Gallup, 22%. Which president owns that dubious honor?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🚨 Don’t Miss It!

Is the Iran ceasefire over? Join Mehdi and Prem TODAY at 11am ET, 8am PT, or 4pm GMT, for Zeteo’s ‘Ask the Editor.’

💬 Quote Unquote

“Yes, antisemitism often cloaks itself in anti-Zionism. So don’t do the antisemites’ work for them. If you keep telling people that if they oppose the Jewish state then they must hate the Jewish people, eventually, they will believe you.”

Ezra Klein, in theNew York Times, on why leftist streamer Hasan Piker “isn’t a ‘Jew hater.’ He’s an anti-Zionist.”

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israeli airstrikes in Khan Younis and northern Gaza killed two Palestinians and injured several others, the Wafa agency said. Middle East Eye found an “organized state policy” of sexual violence against Palestinians in Israeli prisons.

🇭🇺 Orbán out: Far-right Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán was beaten after 16 years in power by Péter Magyar. Magyar’s center-right opposition party, Tisza, was on track to secure a two-thirds majority.

🇳🇬 Village attack: More than 100 people were killed and 35 others were injured in Yobe state as the Nigerian military launched airstrikes on Boko Haram militants, Amnesty said.

🇭🇹 Deadly rush: A stampede broke out at “La Citadelle,” a tourist attraction in northern Haiti, during an annual celebration. At least 30 people were killed.

🙄 WTF?!

Last night, after slamming the Pope, the president of the United States posted this AI-generated image, portraying himself as Jesus, to his Truth Social account. I am trying to imagine the screams of ‘sacrilege’ and ‘blasphemy’ from the right, especially from Christian evangelicals, if Barack Obama or Joe Biden had ever posted such an image of themselves.

🧠 Trivia answer: Harry S. Truman, in February 1952.

📺 Sunday Show Roundup

GOP Rep. Mike Turner claimed Trump’s message to Iran was clear. But when CBS’s Margaret Brennan confronted him with Trump’s statements on the Strait of Hormuz, showing how he flip-flops every couple of days, Turner could not give a straight answer on what Trump’s strategy is to reopen the strait. Turner also failed to respond to whether Congress has proper oversight of the Iran war.

Ahead of Pam Bondi’s scheduled deposition to discuss the Epstein files – which the DOJ says she will not attend – GOP Rep. Byron Donalds said on NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ that the former attorney general shouldn’t have to testify and he would not call for her to be held in contempt – even though he voted to hold Bill and Hillary Clinton in contempt for failing to comply with subpoenas of their own.

Some Democrats want Eric Swalwell to leave Congress, including Rep. Pramila Jayapal, who told NBC’s Kristen Welker she would vote to expel Swalwell, along with GOP Texas Rep. Tony Gonzales, who also faces misconduct allegations, if the House holds a vote on the issue.

Israeli officials are continuing to gaslight the world to rationalize war crimes against Lebanon. “We have no beef with Lebanon. On the contrary, we want to see a thriving Lebanon,” Israeli UN Ambassador Danny Danon told Fox.

🗓️ Mark Your Calendars

Monday, April 13: The heads of the International Energy Agency, International Monetary Fund, and World Bank are set to discuss the energy crisis.

Tuesday, April 14: Bondi is scheduled to appear before the House Oversight Committee for a deposition on her alleged mishandling of the Epstein investigation. The DOJ says she won’t attend.

Wednesday, April 15: White House Budget Director Russell Vought is expected to testify before the House Budget Committee regarding Trump’s 2027 budget request.

Thursday, April 16: NRA annual meetings are set to begin.

Friday, April 17: Trump is set to speak at a Turning Point USA event in Phoenix.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

item.title: What Viktor Orbán’s Landslide Defeat Can Teach Anti-Fascists Across the World
item.description: After 16 straight years in power, Viktor Orbán has finally lost, in a landslide. Young voters in Hungary mobilized in record numbers to vote out the 62-year-old four-term prime minister – and ally of Donald Trump, Benjamin Netanyahu, and Vladimir Putin. Hungary’s youth saw through the…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
After 16 straight years in power, Viktor Orbán has finally lost, in a landslide. Young voters in Hungary mobilized in record numbers to vote out the 62-year-old four-term prime minister – and ally of Donald Trump, Benjamin Netanyahu, and Vladimir Putin. Hungary’s youth saw through the fear-mongering that had buoyed Orbán in election after election since 2010. They want a future different from a fake return to a mythical past.

I once witnessed the future Hungary could have had, and could now have once again. In 2009, I spent two weeks co-directing a summer school on the meaning of the word “if” at Central European University in Budapest, Hungary. Central European University (CEU) was a new university, with a spectacular urban campus in Budapest. Unusually for a European University, its departments were attracting leading professors from around the world. More than 50 graduate students from across the world attended the summer school – of them, about half a dozen are now tenured professors at leading universities, including Princeton, UCLA, Cornell, and Oxford. I met other academics spending the summer in Budapest, attracted by what Sam Gosling, a University of Texas psychology professor whom I met at Corvino, a fashionable Budapest rooftop bar, declared to be “the new Berlin.” We all agreed that the Hungarian capital was destined to be the crown jewel of cosmopolitan Europe, and Central European University had the brightest future of any continental European university.

2009 was an optimistic year. George Bush, the Iraq War, and the financial crisis were (mostly) in the rearview mirror. Barack Obama was the president of the United States. It was a good time to be thinking about the meaning of the word “if,” a concern about as far removed from fascism as I can imagine. I returned to conduct another summer school in Philosophy of Language a year later, in summer 2010.

But in 2010, Budapest had changed. The mood among my Hungarian friends had turned somber since the election of Viktor Orbán and his far-right, Christian nationalist Fidesz party. There was a great deal of talk of increased hostility towards the Roma people, and many grim jokes about politicians’ obsession with restoring Hungary’s past glory, stolen when “greater Hungary” was taken away by an international cabal of globalists with the Treaty of Trianon at the end of World War I. And Central European University was gradually becoming a political target, along with its Jewish “globalist” benefactor, George Soros. CEU was attacked for spreading “Gender Ideology” – the view that gender is a social rather than a biological construct – and other supposed progressive ills.

Return of European Fascism

I was shocked by the change in Budapest. The increasingly autocratic and reactionary Orbán amended the constitution 12 times in his first year in office, before unveiling a brand-new illiberal Hungarian constitution in his second year. I warned my Hungarian friends of the danger that I felt they were in. My own upbringing as the child of a German Jewish father who, as a child, lived through the initial years of the Nazi regime, gave me a background on the surrounding structure and background history of attacks on liberal values, immigrants, intellectuals, Roma people, and LGBTQ citizens. And I set aside work on the formal apolitical areas of philosophy that had defined my work until that point. I turned instead to thinking about the return of European fascism.

I learned much over the ensuing years. I watched as Orbán’s vilification of Central European University transformed into policy. I watched as Hungary used the accreditation system to deny diplomas to its graduates, driving the university out of the country. Through this, I learned that as part of their campaign against “elites,” authoritarians vilify universities, and such vilification has dramatic consequences. I watched and learned from the strategies they employed in destroying democratic institutions – so I was not surprised that in the United States, the Trump regime has focused on accreditation to attack American universities. I was not surprised as the oligarchs Trump owns, such as Larry Ellison, started purchasing the US press, with the help of pressure campaigns by the government. This, too, was simply an emulation of Orbán’s Hungary, where some 80% of the media is now owned by Orbán’s supporters.

Watching my CEU faculty friends’ refusal to confront reality – that they would have to move their families from Hungary to Austria, their kids having to switch from Hungarian language instruction to German halfway through elementary school – I learned much about the power of reality denial during transitions to authoritarianism. I learned that in an authoritarian transition, things are not actually real to you until the second they happen, and perhaps not even then.

Hungary taught me to take authoritarians seriously. This is a lesson I then applied in my work in the United States, where for a decade I have been urging Americans to take the MAGA fascist threat seriously. It was Viktor Orbán’s Hungary that gave me this initial education.

Playbook for MAGA

For the last 16 years, Viktor Orbán has ruled Hungary. Along the way, he has set the playbook for the global fascist right. As with Hitler’s fear of the replacement of Aryans by foreigners, via immigration and race mixing, Orbán made “Great Replacement Theory” central to his politics – going so far as to claim in a speech in 2022 that countries where “races mingle” are no longer nations. Just as Hitler claimed that Jews were responsible for bringing non-white people into Germany, so Orbán claimed that the Jewish billionaire George Soros was funding NGOs whose goal was to bring non-white migrants into Hungary to dilute the purity of the nation.

Running on a platform of “making Hungary great again,” Orbán used patriarchy, xenophobia, homophobia, and racism as tools to rob the Hungarian people of their future. In 2011, Orbán oversaw the passing of a new constitution, Hungary’s Fundamental Law. The Fundamental Law of 2011 sets out a new Hungarian agenda centered on encouraging families to have more children. Article L of the Fundamental Law states:

Hungary shall protect the institution of marriage as the union of one man and one woman established by voluntary decision, and the family as the basis of the survival of the nation. Family ties shall be based on marriage or the relationship between parents and children.

Hungary shall support the commitment to have children.

The protection of children shall be regulated by a cardinal act

In 2010, there were 90,700 births in Hungary. By 2025, 14 years into Hungary’s push to reinvent itself as a nation based on family on children, there were 72,000 live births, the lowest on record. Viktor Orbán ran on warnings of Hungary being overrun by non-white Muslim immigrants, its children being converted to “Gender Ideology” by its best universities. But it is the kleptocracy that seems to accompany the global attacks on wokeness that is instead leading to a Hungarian demographic collapse.

On the Corruption Perception Index, Orbán’s Hungary scores 40 out of 100 (along with Burkina Faso, Cuba, and North Macedonia), ranking as the most corrupt European nation for the fourth consecutive year. Its GDP per capita is among the lowest in the European Union. Excluding oil states, Hungary is only one of two or three high-income states that are non-democratic (along with Singapore and the United States) – and no doubt it is high income mainly because of EU subsidies. Orbán’s Hungary is the paradigmatic example of what my colleague Lucan Way calls “competitive authoritarianism” – a system in which there are competitive elections, but the field is drastically tilted towards the ruling party.

Sixteen years ago, Viktor Orbán promised Hungary’s white Christians a restoration of supposed past glory, with a return to morality and traditional Christian values, and a flowering of the white Hungarian family. What he delivered instead is corruption and economic ruin. The people who benefited from his rule are his family, his friends, and the oligarchs who supported him. Government contracts, taxpayer subsidies, and a tilted legal playing field played in their favor. In exchange, the rest of Hungary’s population was allowed the enjoyment of the humiliation of LGBTQ Hungarians, ethnic minorities, and university professors – a process that has come to be known as “owning the libs.”

Despite being the leader of a small and relatively poor European country, Orbán has been a central figure in the toppling of US democracy. His influence is apparent in ways both large and small. Many Americans know of Orbán’s close relationship with Trump and Vance, Orbán’s influence on Project 2025, the blueprint of the Trump regime, and other very public ways in which Hungarian politics has influenced US fascism. But I have been continually surprised by all of the lesser ways in which Orbán’s Hungary is ubiquitous in American far-right politics.

From Budapest to… Tennessee

In July, 2024, I was teaching at the Critical Race Theory Summer School in Nashville, Tennessee, put on by the African American Policy Forum. I was introduced to Tennessee State Representative Justin Jones, now my friend. As we shook hands for the first time, he asked me, “What do you know about the Danube Institute?” I was startled. What was a 20-something-year-old Tennessee state legislator doing asking me about what is essentially Viktor Orbán’s official government think tank?

It turns out that the Tennessee General Assembly had just discussed a resolution – Senate Joint Resolution 174 – “to recognize and commend the Danube Institute.” I’m guessing that the resolution was not written by anyone in Tennessee – after all, few people in the state are aware that the Danube Institute’s funding came originally from “the Batthyány Lajos Foundation” in 2013, as the resolution notes. What was the Tennessee General Assembly doing voting on a resolution praising Orbán’s think tank, filled with information that, generously speaking, was opaque to those voting on it?

In a video on X posted by the Tennessee Holler, Representative Jones presses the member who introduces the resolution, asking him if he is “familiar with Viktor Orbán.” The person introducing the resolution replies that he is not (he also hilariously mispronounces the name of the Danube Institute). How could a resolution praising Viktor Orbán’s think tank, containing detailed information about its original funding, be introduced by someone who clearly had never heard of Viktor Orbán? Having the kleptocratic tin-pot racist autocrat of an inconsequential European state dominate US politics at every level has been a profound national humiliation for the United States.

We are now all familiar with the global fascist bargain: selling out the economic and political future of the country to oligarchs and autocrats in exchange for state repression of intellectuals, artists, journalists, immigrants, Muslims, atheists, and the LGBTQ community. In the 21st century, in the West, this politics was pioneered in Hungary – padding the pockets of autocrats, their families, and their oligarch supporters in exchange for indulging xenophobia, anti-LGBTQ prejudice, and racism. For a long time, people around the world have seemed content with it – in country after country, people have happily handed over their money and their material prosperity to autocrats and their wealthy supporters in exchange for state policy that targets and vilifies trans women, non-white immigrants, feminists, and university professors.

The End of Orbán

Suddenly, however, in the very country that pioneered the fascist attack on democracy, people have had enough. This election was not a referendum on Orbán’s scapegoats – it was not, for example, a referendum on wokeness. The opposition party leader, Peter Magyar, is himself conservative. He did not run on a pro-LGBTQ platform – rather, he avoided the issue. Magyar also isn’t a fan of migrants or Muslims. Nevertheless, the Hungarian people clearly saw through Orbán’s cynical fear-mongering. Cultural red meat was just no longer relevant in this election – the material conditions of the Hungarian people had just sunk too low. Owning the libs simply no longer could make up for the country’s material diminishment.

Orbán perhaps recognized this himself in this election campaign – he turned instead to a different kind of fear-mongering, about Ukraine, and in particular, Ukraine’s Jewish president, whose face replaced Soros’s in Orbán’s party’s campaign posters and placards. None of it worked – the Hungarian people saw through the façade. We know now that fascist politics is not bound to win elections, that people can see the figures who practice this politics are out to stay in power, at the expense of their nations. Even owning the libs, apparently, can get old.

Hungary is not a democracy – it is a competitive authoritarian state, to quote political scientists Steven Levitsky and Lucan Way. In a competitive authoritarian state, as the United States is becoming, the media is largely under the control of the ruling party, gerrymandering is rife, and the elections are run by members of the ruling party. However, as Levitsky and Way argue, this does not mean that election results are determined in advance. What is required to defeat the ruling party in a competitive authoritarian system is a mass mobilization of voters to the polls, as we have just seen in Hungary. Across the world, we are seeing youth mobilized against fascism and genocide. It looks like this youth energy is what has led at last to Orbán’s defeat.

Fascist politics employs scapegoats to create fear – in the current moment, the scapegoats have been immigrants, Muslims, feminists, and LGBTQ communities (though Orbán has a penchant for adding influential Jewish people to the mix). Magyar avoided addressing these scapegoats, choosing instead to focus on the corruption and self-dealing appeal to scapegoats is intended to conceal. Orbán’s Hungary has been the model for the global fascist assault on democracy. Maybe it can also serve as a model for its defeat.

Jason Stanley is a philosopher and Zeteo contributor. He is also chair of America studies at the University of Toronto, and author of Erasing History: How Fascists Rewrite the Past to Control the Future.

For more of Jason’s writing, subscribe to his Substack, ‘Front Left.’

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.

Check out more from Zeteo:
